- This Tiny Bird's Secret to Joy and Resilience: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Hey there. Have you ever heard of a sunbird? Not the app, not a car. I'm talking about the real feathered living sunbird. These tiny birds are like nature's own jewels. Darting around with colors so bright you'd swear they just flew out of a painting. But their beauty isn't just skin or feather. Deep. There's a lot we can learn from these little dynamos. First off, let's talk about adaptation. Sunbirds are found all over Africa, Asia, and even up into Australia. Some species have long, curving beaks for sipping nectar from deep flowers. Others have evolved shorter, sturdier beaks for different jobs. What does that mean for us? Adaptation is everything. What works in one situation might not in another. So, being flexible like the sunbird gives you a huge advantage. Now, here's something cool. Sunbirds are tiny, but they cover huge distances every day. They don't have size or muscle like an eagle, but they don't let that stop them. Instead, they focus on efficiency. Their wings beat super fast, and they know how to make the most out of every flap. It's a reminder that you don't need to be the biggest or strongest in the room. Sometimes being clever and resourceful is what really counts. Sunbirds are also all about balance. They feed mostly on nectar. Think of them as the hummingbirds of the eastern hemisphere. But they don't just gorge on sugar all day. They mix it up with insects for protein. There's a lesson here. It's easy to get stuck doing what feels comfortable. But real growth happens when you find your balance and try new things. And here's something that just blows my mind. Sunbirds are major pollinators. As they feed, they're unintentionally helping plants reproduce and ecosystems thrive. They might be small, but their impact is massive. So don't ever underestimate the difference you can make, no matter your size or where you come from. To wrap it up, the sunbird teaches us to adapt, to work efficiently, to strike a healthy balance, and to remember that even the smallest among us can have a huge impact. Next time you see a little bird flitting by, think about all the quiet lessons nature's sharing. if you're curious enough to notice. Thanks for hanging out with me. Stay curious, stay adaptable, and I'll see you in the next one.

- Small Actions || Big Impact: Save the Birds, Save Our World || A Path to a Healthier Planet ||: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Birds beautiful diverse and essential to our world from the first light of dawn when their songs greet the day to their graceful flights across the sky Birds bring life and color to our planet they play a crucial role in our ecosystem pollinating plants dispersing seeds and controlling insect populations but despite their resilience and adaptability their lives are in grave danger because of us Birds lead fascinating lives every year millions of them undertake epic Journeys traveling thousands of miles during migration they build intricate nests often in the most unexpected places and care for their young with unwavering dedication air pollution one of the biggest threats to bird populations worldwide the toxic gases released from factories and vehicles pollute the air they breathe birds have delicate respiratory systems and exposure to polluted air can lead to breathing difficulties weakened immune systems and even death the skies that were once their playgrounds are now filled with invisible Killers power lines an unseen and often overlooked Hazard each year millions of birds collide with electrical wires that are nearly invisible to them for many these collisions are fatal high voltage wires can also cause deadly electrocution even wind turbines symbols of renewable energy pose a threat when not designed with bird safety in mind what we see as progress they see as danger habitat destruction is pushing Birds to the brink as forests are cleared and wetlands drained to make way for agriculture and urban expansion birds are losing their homes and food sources but it's not too late to make a change we can take simple steps to protect these beautiful creatures plant trees and shrubs that provide food and shelter support bird friendly designs for wind turbines and make our Windows safer by adding bird-friendly decals we share this planet with birds and countless other species their songs their colors their very existence enrich our lives in ways we often Overlook let's work together to ensure that future Generations can enjoy the beauty and wonder of birds in the wild because when we protect Birds we protect our own future save the birds save the planet

Companies like Helen Energy, TAE Technologies, and Tokamac Energy were being founded. It was during this time that one Richard Dynan founded Applied Fusion Systems or AFS for short. Dynan was an entrepreneur who had first come to fame by starring in two seasons of Made in Chelsea, a reality TV show here in the UK about affluent 20somes. He had a passion for fusion and his company sought to attract investment for research and development into advanced materials and technologies with the overall goal of making a fusion power plant. That is its shining feature. The amazing efficiency of a fusion thruster that would allow the vehicle to do extremely long engine burns to change its velocity far more than other types of rocket engines. That allows for dramatically reduced travel times, cutting weeks or months off of trips to Mars, Jupiter, and beyond. Now, if you've been watching this channel for a while, you might have already spotted a slightly important thing that Sunbird is lacking. Radiators. Waste heat from electronics and big hot fusion engines is really difficult to get rid of in space because it's a vacuum. You know that thing used in thermos flasks to slow down heat transfer. So the only way to get rid of heat in space is through radiation where thermal energy turns into photons which are emitted away. A low heat load can be slowly emitted by the skin of a spacecraft. But radiation is slow. So a high heat load needs specialized radiators that are optimized for the task. But those are totally missing on Sunbird, at least as it currently stands. The next problem is the very optimistic electrical power generation numbers provided. Now, this is possible to do. I mean, fusion power plants are like the whole reason the technology is being developed right now. And well, a fusion reactor needs energy to run in the first place, but the site lacks specifics as to how Sunbird does this or how it gets stored. And there's some solid numbers here demonstrating just how much more useful having a highly capable tug waiting in space really is. Well, when I say solid, I mean wrong. Let's go back up the page a bit and look at these numbers here. These are all delta V or change in velocity. It's how much a spacecraft can speed up or slow down by. As this page says, you got to change your velocity by around 9,400 m/s to get up to orbit since you got to fall so fast sideways you miss the ground. Then these other numbers are how much you need to change your velocity to go to other places like Mars. But hang on, these numbers don't match up. In the first paragraph, it's saying you need an additional 1.9 km/s to go to Mars or an additional 5 km/s for Jupiter. Both of which are too low. And then in the second paragraph for the same journeys, there are totally different but at least more correct numbers. And then lower down the page there's another third set of numbers which is it. It's not just for these two trips either. The values for going to a space station around the moon are simultaneously too high and inconsistent. This number is closer to what you need to land on the moon, not orbit around it. You know what this reminds me of? the way large language models like chat GPT aren't good at numbers because they cannot do math. That's not how they work. They're just good at predicting the next thing to say. I have no idea if this is what has been used to write the website. But what is clear is that the proof reading here is not great. Just like that long live typo on AFS's website. Yeah, see it was going somewhere. But we're not done with Sunbird just yet. While reading the lunar supply run bit, there's an odd mention of using the oirth effect. This is the counterintuitive way that doing a rocket burn at higher speeds creates a greater change in velocity. It's a handy trick. It means it's most efficient to do engine burns at the lowest point on your orbit. Works great for really high thrust chemical rockets where burns might take minutes at most, but is not so great for super low thrust fusion drives that might need to burn for hours or days. Yeah, it would use a lot less mass of propellant in that time, but it's hardly fast, especially for trips to the moon. You can lose money, and it's still a successful mission if I've saved you time. Next up is the radiation shielding the craft is supposed to have because this sentence here just reads like technobl. The space environment is a tough one in terms of radiation for all of these things this other sentence says. But this whole text box really undersells the dangers of radiation from the fusion reactors. Without tons and tons of shielding, the payload would be being blasted with neutrons that would irradiate it, imbrit it, and severely harm anything biological. And yes, there would be neutrons emitted even from the supposedly autonic helium 3 fusion that Sunbird is meant to use. Quick fusion lesson. Atoms made of protons and neutrons in the nucleus. Number of protons determines the element. Electrons whiz around outside. Hydrogen has one proton. Dutyium, it's just hydrogen with a neutron hanging out with the proton. Tritium, still hydrogen, but there's two neutrons at the party with the proton. Helium 3. Helium has two protons. The three means there's a neutron in there with them. Fusion is smashing atoms together so hard they mix together, which needs a lot of energy. The easiest nuclear reaction with the lowest energy requirement is DT, dutyium, and tritium. They fuse together and make helium 4. You can figure that one out, but have a spare neutron which goes off and causes mischief. This is all that stuff I mentioned a minute ago. It goes and joins up with a stable atom and makes it unstable and radioactive or smacks atoms out of place which weakens material and causes havoc to living tissue. All around bad stuff. That's why you need shielding. The next fusion reaction up which needs a bit more energy is D&D. Not that one or that one. Dutyium and dutyium. These fuse into one of two things. Around half turns into tritium and hydrogen. And the other around half turns into helium 3 and a neutron. And this is really awkward because now you've got DT going on as well, spitting out its own neutrons everywhere. This is important to bear in mind for the next level up. And this is where we find the fusion Sunbird is supposed to do. Dutyium and helium 3. This needs more energy to do than the other two and outputs helium 4 and a lone proton. That's it. No nasty neutrons causing problems, right? Well, the issue is that some of that dutyium hanging around in the reactor ends up getting fused with itself. And as we know, DD makes neutrons and things that also make neutrons. But there's barely any mention of this issue and what they're doing about it on their website. At least the basic idea of using the protons from DH3 for space propulsion makes sense. That's something, right? Where's all that helium 3 going to come from, though? In various interviews, Richard Dyn makes a big point about being able to charge people for this rare and expensive fusion fuel. >> So, I can charge you for your helium 3 where I couldn't do that on Earth. >> Though, doesn't have much to say about sourcing it beyond breeding it. But where in the Sunbirds or in some other space nuclear reactors? I'd like a little more specificity here since having a good source of helium 3 is kind of needed if you want to use it up to fly places. That's why Helicity Space is looking more closely at DD Fusion for its fusion drive. This is from a scientific article by that company's co-founder and chief scientist. By the way, with all that said, let's recap Sunbird with a handy checklist. Thermal management, no. Power generation, it says so, but not how. Real numbers, no. Some of them are wrong. Shielding, yeah, it's there, but I don't think it's enough and it's kind of technobly. Fuel source, kind of handwaved. scientific articles. I couldn't find any. There's not any linked on their websites like how Helicity Space does it, but I also didn't see any in search results. Doesn't mean they don't exist, though. So, to conclude, in my opinion, the Sunbird concept seems wildly optimistic. Not only is the concept lacking important details behind how it's supposed to work, it's lacking whole key features like radiators. To be fair here, final vehicle stuff does end up looking decently different to earlier concepts. Just look at the progression made by SpaceX from the BFR concepts to Starship. At least Pulsar Fusion does have experience and knowledge for making and testing space hardware. Their Hall effect thrusters may not be a super exciting space technology, but they do apparently have clients buying these and the backing of the UK space agency to develop bigger ones. And this is great. Developing the UK space industry is fantastic. I love to see it. But the company isn't called Pulsar Thrusters. It's called Pulsar Fusion. I don't get this weird gap between what they've actually been making over the years and what they're supposedly aiming for. Eh, whatever. I guess we'll see this year because their timeline for Sunbird says static tests are going to happen in advance of an inorbit demonstration of core technology components in 2027. In my opinion, given the strange path that the company is taking to get there, it might be a bit longer before we see Sunbird fly, if it happens at all. Alternatively, you can support us directly through YouTube by becoming a channel member. Thanks to our supporters and thank you for watching.

- शकरखोरा चिड़िया के बारे में जानकारी || Information About Purple Sunbird || Cinnyris Asiaticus: Kind: captions Language: en Hello friends, welcome to your own YouTube channel, Zee Premi. Today we are going to share information about the Sugar Sunbird. This small, beautiful bird is known by various names like Sarkar Khora, Full Moon, Sun Bird, etc. in Hindi, and Purple Sunbird in English. What's the problem with its zoological name? Sugar Sunbird is found in India and the Indian subcontinent, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, and Myanmar. Sugar Sunbird is a perennial bird of India, found throughout India up to 5,000 feet in the Himalayas. It is seen in fields, gardens, flowerbeds, and rural and urban areas. The Sugar Sunbird is a small, beautiful bird. The male and female Sugar Sunbird are similar in size but differ in color. The male is a bright dark blue-purple color, which appears shiny from a distance. It has silky yellow and red feathers under its feet, which attracts the female. The female's upper body is olive brown and the underside is yellow. The beak is black, long, and slants downward. Its eyes are black, and its claws are also black. The gull is 10 centimeters long. Its diet is primarily honey mixed with pollen, which it sucks with its thin, slightly curved beak. Its tube-like jaws are also designed to suck flower nectar. It inserts its beak into flowers and sucks the nectar, helping the flowers to reproduce. It also eats insects and spiders. It has a fast, direct flight, but it hovers and sucks the nectar from flowers. The gull's breeding season is from March to June. It builds its nest in small trees and sometimes even in human homes. Its nest is cup-shaped, and is neatly constructed from grass fibers and spider webs. The female does the nesting, which takes 5 to 10 days. The female gull lays two to three eggs at a time, which are gray in color. The female hatches eggs. The chicks hatch in 15 to 17 days. They come out of the eggs and after the chicks come out of the eggs, it tells them about their share in the responsibilities like feeding them and teaching them to fly. The lifespan of a sugar-eater bird is about 22 years. Tell us by commenting how you liked the information given by us and if you liked the information given by us then like our video and subscribe to our channel for information about such animals and first of all press the like button on all after half an hour of watching our video. - Inside the Illegal Songbird Trade in Indonesia: Kind: captions Language: en The first time I visited one of these big bird markets, I was just shocked and astounded to see the volume of birds that were in the market and the number of different species. The songbird black market is immense, it crosses every border, it's across the globe. We really felt that it was so important to actually start working with some of these species before it was too late before they were driven over the precipice and became extinct. The ones in Jakarta, particularly the one called Pramuka Bird Market which is actually the biggest wildlife market on the planet is a really depressing place to visit actually as someone who's a bird lover, someone that's very passionate about conservation. Unless we can control and stop the illegal wildlife trade in these songbirds the inevitable result is going to be the extinction of multiple species across Southeast Asia. So these forests really are falling silent. So I've worked in the zoo community as a aviculturist, if you like a bird enthusiast, for many many years. Conservation has always been the driving force behind everything that I do. And in Indonesia in particular, the songbird crisis is at such a height that I felt that we really needed to do as much as we possibly can to try to protect these wonderful endemic species. I've been working within Indonesia with partner organizations, conservationists for a number of years trying to save those species that have been driven to the edge of extinction through the illegal wildlife trade. As you walk in through these dark damp corridors, the initial thing that hits you is the smell the smell of birds and bird mess, bird feces and under every corner there's a little shady bird shop where there's birds cramped into tiny little cages under the covers in dark corners. There's often dead birds in the gutter where they've thrown out to feed the cats of birds or having survived over the night. These birds are trapped by local trappers and then they go to a middleman who trades the birds across the whole Indonesian archipelago, these birds may have to travel for many days over, even thousands of miles to get there. The vast majority of these are songbirds. They are very unique in their adaptations are amazing. They have beautiful colors, generally, many of them are a bright green, some are red. They are very often, of course have very lovely songs. And those are the ones that are most highly prized the birds that have incredible voices. So one element of the bird keeping industry within Indonesia is the bird singing competitions, and these are incredible events to see to witness. At least competitions there'll be many people there, all of the bird keepers will be hanging their birds up under a large canopy there'll be 30 or 40 birds in each competition. It's a really frenzied environment where all of the bird owners are screaming and shouting at their birds to try to encourage them to sing louder. The value of these prizes is incredibly high for the top champion. The top champion white rump Sharma, for example, the prize could be something like $50,000 for the winner, or occasionally, we've heard rumors that the winner will win a house or a big fancy motorcar. Very often the wild caught birds are the highest in demand if you like they're the birds that they really want so they always feel that the wild caught birds have more vigor than the captured birds. That sort of fuels the trade in these illegally caught birds rather than focusing on the captive bred bird. If the bird is a protected bird, he will use the ring. So some of those birds have leg bands on, leg rings on on which is which are able to identify them as captive bred, there is an element of captive breeding going on, but they also sometimes put leg bands onto birds so the clearly wild caught. These birds are really important to the ecosystem within Southeast Asia. Many of the birds are fruit eater so they're very important seed dispersers. They're also very important pollinators as well. There may be elements here where the insectivores play a role in terms of pest control within the environment. So they're extremely important. It's actually very difficult to rescue or confiscate birds from the markets, but when we do they end up in our conservation breeding center partners, where they're able to breed and reproduce and with hopefully with the ultimate aim of getting these birds back out into the wild. But of course it's very important that we have safe secure environments where we can do so. So we have to work and we want to work with local communities, so that we can create nice environments for the wildlife but also for the local people in Indonesia, as well. And we're looking at alternative livelihoods for these people. So, the bird trappers, we want to change their mindsets and really give them alternative occupations or livelihoods so that they don't need to rely on catching birds from the wild. The main sort of ambition if you like is really to try to have bird watchers of the future rather than bird keepers right so that's a long term aim but hopefully we'll get there in the future.

- Feathers in Flight: The Bird Genoscape Project | National Geographic: Kind: captions Language: en-US ♪♪♪ (Mary) We are on the Kern River Preserve. It's beautiful to walk on the preserve this time of year. The mornings are really cool. This time of year is also amazing because you're hearing all the birdsong earlier in the morning. The Willow Flycatcher is this small, plain bird. And it flies around and catches flies, because that’s the type of insect it eats the most. And it nests near rivers or creeks. Knowing their vocalizations is key to doing the research because otherwise, you'd rarely see them if you didn't. They say what the flycatchers do is "fitz-bew." You wouldn’t even call it a song, really. I mean it’s, it's okay. It doesn't sound bad. I love it when I hear it, because it means there's a Willow Flycatcher nearby. So, definitely. I’ve lived here in the Kern River Valley since the winter of 1990. When I first started working here, there were more Willow Flycatchers. And now it's down to about, as far as we know at this point in time, 1 individual. That's all we know of right now and it's kind of depressing. (Steve) As a whole, Empidonax traillii, the Willow Flycatcher, is not endangered. In fact, it's relatively abundant in North America. However, the subspecies that inhabits the southwestern United States is federally endangered because of rapid and steep population declines over the latter half of the 20th century. Basically, I view the Willow Flycatcher as a canary in a coalmine. It's not doing well, so why is it not doing well here? And if you can solve that for it’s probably going to prevent other bird species from having the same problems later on. (Kristen) In the U.S., there's roughly 100 species of neotropical migratory bird that are either threatened, endangered or a species of special concern. A neotropical migrant is a bird that breeds in North America and then migrates south of the US border, into the neotropics. Before I really began to study migration in detail, I tended to think of these birds as our birds. They’re in the North American field guide, they must be North American birds, right? But they spend, in most cases, more than half the year south of the US border. Of the nearly 800 species of birds that breed in North America, about half migrate into the neotropics. It's just a, it’s a huge number. So if you observe that a migratory bird species is declining, one of the first questions you have to ask is whether it's declining due to factors on the breeding grounds in North America, the wintering grounds in the neotropics, or somewhere in between, or a combination of those. The main reason why it's hard to figure out why some of these species are in trouble is because they're only in our backyards for, say, 3 months of the year. And the rest of the time, we don't know where they're going. So we don't know what threats they're facing in those areas. In order to develop conservation strategies, we need to be able to identify how breeding and wintering populations are linked. So migratory bird conservation has to be a collaborative effort with biologists all over Latin America. (Natalie) [speaking Spanish] Yes, taking birds out of the net is one of my favorite things [speaking Spanish] because you can learn a lot of new information [speaking Spanish] when you have a little bird in your hands. [speaking Spanish] In ornithology, yes, "a bird in the hand is worth more than 100 in the bush [speaking Spanish] That's the saying, yes! (Chespi) More than 220 species of birds actually migrate, mostly from North America, to Costa Rica. Birds know no borders. They don't understand these boundaries between countries. They simply use ecosystems. There are huge networks of people that do bird banding all over the western hemisphere. So you capture the birds in a mist net and you put a little metal leg band on the bird. And then you hope to recapture that bird at some other point in its annual cycle. But the recapture rates are incredibly low. They're less than 1%. So our thought was, well if we could develop a technology that could use information within a single feather, like information in the DNA of the bird, to track their migratory movements, then we can take a feather collected from anywhere along the migratory journey and link it back to which breeding population that came from. The Bird Genoscape Project is an effort to use genomic sequencing to understand the population- specific movements of migratory birds. The original idea came from the co-director of the Bird Genoscape Project, Tom Smith, who literally just decided about 20 years before the technology was actually available that we should be able to do this. (Tom) In the early 90s, I naively thought that well, if we just collect some feathers we’ll be able to get DNA out of feathers, and it took the Human Genome Project to really develop the tools to enable us to begin to link populations. So I live in Colorado now, but if I wanted to know where my ancestors came from, I might send my DNA off to a genetic ancestry service. They would compare my DNA against this huge database of DNA from many, many other people and be able to identify that most of my ancestry is from Norway, which is where I know my grandparents came from. We can do the same thing with the Bird Genoscape Project. We collaborate with these bird banding stations, which are all over North America and Latin America, to collect feathers. And then our first step is essentially creating that ancestry database. When we sequence the genome from birds all across the breeding range, we use that to build a map of how the different birds are related to one another and what the unique, genetically unique populations are across the breeding range. And then we can take a feather collected from outside of its breeding range and we can compare that with the expected genetic profile of birds from different parts of the breeding range. And we can tell you that this bird is actually a bird that might be found breeding in the Sierra Nevada mountains, or New York, or something like that. Which is really cool! My specific role in the Bird Genoscape Project is collecting genetic samples of Willow Flycatchers throughout their wintering range. And then also the breeding grounds as well. My role is the fun stuff, I think! [birds chirping] I have a speaker and I'm going to use it to do some playback of the Willow Flycatcher vocalizations in order to lure it into the net. Ok. And I use a long cord so I’m a good distance away from the net and I won't scare them. Where’d it go? Yep, that’s him! He was a feisty one, but we got him. So this is a Willow Flycatcher, and he’s not happy. He’s a very feisty guy. Yeah. Here we go. OK, so the first thing I'm going to do is one of the most important things is to have the genetic samples. So I'm going to pluck the tail feather first. Sorry, guy. The general sort of description of where the willow flycatcher winters from bird books is from Mexico down to the northern tip of South America. When the Southwestern Willow Flycatcher was listed as endangered, we had no idea where this particular subspecies wintered. Did it go throughout that whole range? Or just a portion of the range? We had no idea. Once we have developed what we called the Genoscape the map of how populations are related on the breeding grounds we can take any individual captured on the wintering areas or anywhere along its migratory trajectory and tell you where that bird was from. So the end product of the Bird Genoscape Project for a particular species is the creation of a map that details the genetically distinct lineages and where they're found on the breeding range. Similar to the kind of map that you would find in a bird book. But in this case, it's population-specific. And then it also details where those different populations, which migratory routes they use and then where they're found on the wintering grounds. The Bird Genoscape Project was able to show us with high precision where the wintering areas of the Southwestern Willow Flycatcher are. So people have dedicated their lives to knowing where this bird spends the winter And we know now that it winters in specific areas in Costa Rica and Nicaragua. So now we can really focus our efforts to where they're needed, which is super cool. That's the whole point! Right now we have, just for the neotropical migrants, about 200,000 feathers. So that represents roughly around 100 or so species. Right now, we're working on roughly 20 species of migratory birds and they're in various stages of development. Our long-term goal is to map 100 species of migratory birds of higher conservation concern by the year 2025. The collaboration is growing. If we go back to the early 90s, it was myself and my graduate students in my lab. And now, you know, we have many, many collaborators from across North America and Central America. We know where the birds are and where to get the samples. What we're doing right now is just adding one extra field, which is collect a feather and send it back to the States. For us, that's very little. But for the birds, that’s huge. And that's our win-win relationship with the Bird Genoscape Project. The ultimate goal is really creating a network a franchise, if you will, of different universities, different research groups, all coming together to solve these mysteries of migratory birds. That’s my hope. I think the willow flycatcher is a fantastic example of why the work is so important. It enables conservation to be targeted to those places in most need. If the Southwestern Willow Flycatcher went extinct, I think a lot of people, they’d shrug their shoulders and say well, that doesn't matter a whole lot. But I think the decline of the Southwestern Willow Flycatcher is kind of an alarm call. And if it disappears, it's a huge warning that there's something wrong. [speaking Spanish] Birds give us [speaking Spanish] that kind of sountrack [speaking Spanish] that song that's present in many of our stories. [speaking Spanish] I can't imagine how the world would be without bird songs. [speaking Spanish] It's part of being in the world and being alive, I think. [speaking Spanish] It's part of us. I think birds bring people together. It is like when you go to another country and you realize that someone speaks the same language, and you can have like endless conversations just because you speak the same language. For us, it's the language of bird conservation. I think one of the great outcomes of the project is connecting the people who are working on the same birds, but in different parts of the world at different seasons. in a way that couldn’t happen before, because we didn’t know where the birds were going. That makes me really happy. It’s the same birds in your backyard in Guatemala that are in my backyard in Colorado. We’re all working toward the same goal with the same species, and it just, it couldn't be more clear that we all need to be on the same page working together.

DIRECTOR (in Japanese to the interpreter): The translation is very important, O.K.? The translation.
INTERPRETER: Yes, of course. I understand.
DIRECTOR: Mr. Bob-san. You are sitting quietly in your study. And then there is a bottle of Suntory whiskey on top of the table. You understand, right? With wholehearted feeling, slowly, look at the camera, tenderly, and as if you are meeting old friends, say the words. As if you are Bogie in ''Casablanca,'' saying, ''Cheers to you guys,'' Suntory time!
INTERPRETER: He wants you to turn, look in camera. O.K.?
BOB: That's all he said?
INTERPRETER: Yes, turn to camera.
BOB: Does he want me to, to turn from the right or turn from the left?
INTERPRETER (in very formal Japanese to the director): He has prepared and is ready. And he wants to know, when the camera rolls, would you prefer that he turn to the left, or would you prefer that he turn to the right? And that is the kind of thing he would like to know, if you don't mind.
DIRECTOR (very brusquely, and in much more colloquial Japanese): Either way is fine. That kind of thing doesn't matter. We don't have time, Bob-san, O.K.? You need to hurry. Raise the tension. Look at the camera. Slowly, with passion. It's passion that we want. Do you understand?
INTERPRETER (In English, to Bob): Right side. And, uh, with intensity.
BOB: Is that everything? It seemed like he said quite a bit more than that.
DIRECTOR: What you are talking about is not just whiskey, you know. Do you understand? It's like you are meeting old friends. Softly, tenderly. Gently. Let your feelings boil up. Tension is important! Don't forget.
INTERPRETER (in English, to Bob): Like an old friend, and into the camera.
BOB: O.K.
DIRECTOR: You understand? You love whiskey. It's Suntory time! O.K.?
BOB: O.K.
DIRECTOR: O.K.? O.K., let's roll. Start.
BOB: For relaxing times, make it Suntory time.
DIRECTOR: Cut, cut, cut, cut, cut! (Then in a very male form of Japanese, like a father speaking to a wayward child) Don't try to fool me. Don't pretend you don't understand. Do you even understand what we are trying to do? Suntory is very exclusive. The sound of the words is important. It's an expensive drink. This is No. 1. Now do it again, and you have to feel that this is exclusive. O.K.? This is not an everyday whiskey you know.
INTERPRETER: Could you do it slower and ----
DIRECTOR: With more ecstatic emotion.
INTERPRETER: More intensity.
DIRECTOR (in English): Suntory time! Roll.
BOB: For relaxing times, make it Suntory time.
DIRECTOR: Cut, cut, cut, cut, cut! God, I'm begging you.

- Birds evolution history: Kind: captions Language: en weeklycross.com. >> Marco, you spent years traveling the world just to photograph birds. What was the most surprising discovery from all those trips? >> The nature is a great copycat. Look at this photo here. I took it in Kenya in the African savannah. >> Beautiful vibrant yellow. That black V on the chest. It looks a lot like a bird I saw in a documentary about the prairies of the United States. >> Exactly. This is the yellowthroated long claw which lives in Africa. And the American one you saw was probably the eastern meolark. They're practically identical. Same colors, same plumage pattern, same behavior. They live in open fields, sing from high perches, feed on insects on the ground. >> So they're close relatives. >> That's the great mystery. They're not. The longclaw belongs to the pippet and wagtail family. The meadowark is related to blackbirds and orioles. The last time they shared a common ancestor was over 50 million years ago. Lanaeus, the father of modern taxonomy, was so confused that he originally thought they were the same species. >> How is it possible for them to look so similar without being related? >> Convergent evolution. Similar environments create similar solutions. The open grasslands of Africa and the Americas shaped these birds in almost identical ways completely independently. >> And how are these species doing today in terms of conservation? >> The African longclaw is doing relatively well classified as least concern. But the American meadowark, it has lost 89% of its population since 1970. Changes in farming practices, pesticide use, habitat loss. It's frightening to watch such a common bird disappear like this. >> And the shiny little one here. >> Ah, this one is very special. A sunbird. I photographed it in Tanzania near the Angoro Angoro crater. Does it remind you of anything? >> A hummingbird? They must be related. >> Everyone thinks that at first glance. Sunbirds and hummingbirds occupy exactly the same ecological role. They pollinate tubular flowers, have stunning iridescent plumage, long curved bills, but hummingbirds are exclusive to the Americas and can hover in the air like helicopters. Sunbirds live in Africa and Asia, and they need to perch to feed. >> And genetically, what's the relationship? >> Completely different. Hummingbirds are related to swifts. Sunbirds are closer to crows. Evolution invented the same solution twice on continents separated by oceans. >> Incredible. Do you have more examples like this? >> This one is my absolute favorite, the secretary bird. I photographed it in South Africa. 5t tall, elegant, long legs, and it kills venomous snakes with powerful kicks. >> It looks like an elegant dinosaur with feathers. >> And in South America, we have the sera. Same size, same long legs, same brutal hunting method. But the secretary bird is related to eagles and hawks. The serma, a distant relative of falcons and parrots. And there's an even more fascinating connection. The serma is the closest living relative of the terror birds, those giant 10-foot predators that dominated South America millions of years ago. >> And what about their conservation status? >> The secretary bird was reclassified as endangered in 2020. Habitat loss, urbanization, human disturbance. The Brazilian sera is safer for now, but it also faces growing pressure from intensive agriculture. >> So birds that look like identical twins can have completely different fates. >> Exactly. Appearances are deceiving. Evolution created these perfect doubles over millions of years. But what determines survival today isn't their shape. It's where they live, how we adapt to them, and whether we choose to protect them or not. It's as if nature has been showing us repeatedly what works and we decide whether to pay attention or ignore the lesson. Couldn't have said it better.

- How Weaver Birds Evolved to Build Huge Nests: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] the rising sun marks the start of a new day and in africa's kalahari desert the wildlife is already awake and busy this masked weaver bird scans the area carefully collecting blades of dry grass to build his nest most birds simply build what looks like a loose bowl of twigs but this is a weaver bird and like his namesake he weaves his nests into intricate structures these chambers hang from trees an impressive feat of avian architecture their cousins the sociable weaver birds take nest building even further together our colony will build a huge compound nest containing more than 100 chambers each one housed under a single roof it's a bird apartment complex one of the world's largest nests and it isn't just for breeding weavers live in them throughout the year some nests lasting for even a century as it passes from generation to generation to craft his nest the weaver bird loops together blades of grass around a tree branch having created the base he then builds the hollow body before adding the entrance last he'll need around a thousand blades to make a strong nest and this may take him a few weeks to complete some species design domes while others prefer tubed entrances but nests of the sociable weaver birds aren't made in the same way they don't so much weave them rather they stack them with whatever plant material they can find true weaver birds are only found in the subfamily placini in the whole weaver family there are over 100 species most of which live in sub-saharan africa the family originated in the mid myosin around 15 million years ago it was a time when african ecosystems made the shift from forests to grasslands allowing early weavers to spread and radiate into the species seen today although some aren't true weavers they can all build elaborate nests and the journey of how this behavior emerged may be simpler than expected the weaver family belongs to a group of birds known as passerines most passerines today build open nests and it could easily be assumed that some birds then learn to enclose them but this study found it was actually the other way around open nests evolved from closed nests in ancient passerines even occurring independently at least three times they're simple and easy to build and they don't cost as much energy and yet the weaver birds still kept their roofs in the kalahari temperatures can climb to 110 degrees fahrenheit during the day and dip by 40 at night enclosed nests offer more protection from these weather extremes preventing birds and their eggs from reaching deadly temperatures they also free parents from the burden of incubation as they no longer need to provide constant shade for the young themselves and suffer under the blazing sun for they are stable homes in fact they may be so well adapted for extreme conditions that they could have restricted their dispersal as weavers are mostly found in hot dry climates other passerine birds expanded into a wide range of habitats losing the domes and opting for a more freeing nest shape in early weavers solitary nesting may have then led to group nesting this may be seen in the placer passerini subfamily where the sporophytes mostly breed alone while the other genera breed in colonies it isn't uncommon for small birds to live in groups they flock together moving to wherever there's food or water in groups information can be easily exchanged between individuals allowing birds to follow whoever's managed to find food and this can be useful especially when food appears in abundant patches weaver birds mostly feed on insects and since there's enough to go around group living can be favored it can also reduce the risk of predation small birds tend to have many natural enemies and so the larger the colony the more eyes on the lookout and the lower the chances of being targeted in weavers selection may have driven larger groups but when there are too many in a single tree it can get a little crowded physical contact between nests occurs and eventually some nests are even built directly next to others fused together into a single mass usually birds and colonies are still territorial they can get aggressive defending their space by pecking and squawking at the intruder but an enclosed nest helps to more easily define the boundaries of a territory and allows birds to tolerate their neighbors even in tight masses this may have been an important precursor for the evolution of compound nesting in greycap's social weavers birds may build both separate and compound nests so they may represent an intermediate stage towards this behavior but sociable weavers differ in that they also build a communal roof over their nests the roof requires colony members to work together to maintain this behavior was long thought to be driven by the extra security from predators but predation rates are still high in fact on average 70 of clutches are often raided especially from snakes so perhaps this roof building behavior was driven by temperature control instead some studies have even suggested it's maintained by kin selection sociable weavers are very cooperative birds nearly all breeding pairs are assisted by helpers juveniles will also help feed their younger siblings and even neighbours and because close neighbours tend to be relatives building a communal roof may be driven by kin selection since it benefits the extended family as well not all birds contribute to the job equally but those that do are more closely related to colony members than those that don't in weavers parallel evolution of compound nesting has even occurred across the different subfamilies and convergent evolution across different species highlights the significance of this behavior in small birds these nests are a work of animal architecture capturing beautifully how nature strives for survival in the most magnificent of ways you

- Hummingbird Nest: The Cost of Staying Alive | Wildlife Documentary | BBTV Official: Kind: captions Language: en Most hummingbird nests fail without ever being noticed. We are often amazed by hummingbirds. Small birds with bright feathers and wings that blur when they move. They hover in the air, shift fast, and seem light, almost free. But that beauty is only what we see on the surface. What keeps them alive is not found in flight or speed or color. In this film from BBTV official, we move away from the air and into a much smaller space where survival depends on quiet work, narrow limits, and constant risk. This is the story of the hummingbird's nest. Hummingbirds are often remembered for how they look. Small bodies, bright colors, wings that blur in the light. They hover in place, then slide away, barely touching the air, never staying long enough to hold the frame. In the sun, their feathers flash and fade. green, red, gold, gone in a second. Everything about them feels light, thin, easy to miss, as if weight never fully settles on the body. They seem soft, almost unreal, like something passing through the scene rather than living inside it. Those familiar images are not wrong, but they are not enough. They do not show what the bird depends on to stay alive. They do not explain how such a small body lasts day after day. What we see feels complete. Yet something is missing. Beauty holds our attention while survival happens somewhere else. Survival does not live in flight. It does not sit in speed, motion, or in the blur of wings. It appears when everything slows down, when the air stops moving, when the body rests and nothing pulls the eye, there is no color flash, no sharp turn, no sign that anything important is taking place. The moment feels empty, easy to pass over. Yet, this is where survival sits. When nothing seems to be happening, what keeps the bird alive does not shine or move. It hides in places the eye skips. Small moments, quiet choices, narrow limits. Nothing here looks strong or important. Yet this is where balance is held. Miss it and everything else falls apart. This story does not begin in the air. It begins somewhere small, pulled away from open view, close to still branches and quiet corners. A space narrow enough to miss, hidden among leaves and shade, where nothing seems to happen inside a structure smaller than a fist. The nest is not just a place to stay. It is a system built to keep life going, to protect what is most fragile. It is almost invisible at first glance, small enough to blend into branches, leaves, and shade, easy to pass without notice. Its size feels minor, even fragile when set against the open space around it. The wind, the height, the moving world beyond. Yet inside this small space, everything that matters is held together, shaped to keep something alive that cannot protect itself. The contrast is quiet but clear. A structure so small, carrying a role so large, working without drawing attention. And everything about it is limited. The nest is small. So small there is no room for error. Every part has to fit. The body, the eggs, the space they will grow into. Nothing can be extra. Nothing can be wasted. The walls sit close. The floor holds only what is needed. And every move is made within tight limits. There is no space to shift later. No margin to adjust once a choice is made. What does not fit cannot stay. And what is placed wrong cannot be fixed. The size forces care, forces precision, forces restraint. Because here, even the smallest change carries weight, which makes every choice matter. The materials come from what is already there. Spider silk, thin plant fibers, bits of lychen taken one trip at a time. Nothing is picked by chance. Each piece is chosen for what it can do. The silk holds, stretches, pulls things together. The fibers link and shape. The soft layers help everything stay in place. The bird returns again and again, carrying almost nothing each time, adding one small part, then another. Each trip looks minor, easy to ignore. But every piece has a role. Still, none of them works alone. No strand, no fiber, no layer can hold the nest by itself. But no single piece makes a difference. Each return changes almost nothing. The nest looks the same. The shape holds. The size does not grow in a way the eye can track. [music] The bird comes back, then leaves, then comes back again, carrying little, adding less than [music] expected. From one moment to the next, there is no clear progress, no sign that work is being done. The nest stays [music] still, quiet, unchanged. as if time is not moving at all. But the returns continue, steady, even, without pause. Each visit laying down another layer of effort that does not show right away. The change lives in repetition, not in results, in time spent rather than distance covered until return becomes the work itself. The shape of the nest does not appear all at once. It is pressed, pushed, bent into place through small repeated movements. The bird leans in, turns slightly, presses its body against the soft walls, then pulls away and does it again. Each touch leaves a mark. Each pause sets the form a little more. The body becomes the mold using weight and heat to guide the curve to set the depth to hold the edge. There is no clear step where the shape is finished. Only contact after contact, pressure after pressure, building form through use. What holds the nest together comes from this closeness. body to fiber, motion to surface. Nothing added from a distance. Nothing here is meant to stand alone. No part here is strong on its own. Each strand depends on another. Each layer holds only because something else holds with it. Strength comes from connection, from pieces crossing and locking together in ways that cannot be pulled apart one by one. Remove a single link and the balance shifts. Pull too hard on one point and the whole form begins to give. What keeps the nest standing is not force, but how many small parts lean on each other at the same time. That shared hold is what lets it last, which also makes it fragile. After many days, the nest is finished, but all of that work rests on a single thin branch. The shape holds. The pieces stay in place. The balance feels steady for now. Yet nothing here is secured beyond this point. The weight, the angle, the quiet hold of the branch keep everything suspended in a narrow state between staying and falling. There is no backup, no second support, no way to spread the risk. What was built to protect now depends on stillness, on balance, on forces that can change without warning. The work is complete, but safety is not. And once it is finished, everything depends on what happens next. Finishing the nest does not mean safety. It only marks a change. The work stops, but the pressure does not. What felt like an ending turns into a new stage, one with fewer choices and less room to act. The nest now stays in place, open to the air, fixed to its branch, unable to move or adjust. What was built to protect becomes something that can be seen, reached, tested. The risk does not fade. It shifts, settling in, and growing quieter, harder to escape. None of these are enemies. None act with intent or warning. They arrive without sound, without shape, without a clear moment to respond. Each one alone can be endured, but together they press from different sides, changing balance, timing, and control. What was stable becomes uncertain, not through force, but through quiet pressure that does not stop. And none of it can be controlled. Everything has to be right. Nothing needs to be touched. Nothing needs to be taken. Pressure builds from presence alone, from the chance of being noticed, from timing that slips for just a second. Some losses leave no sound, no clear moment to point at. One day, the nest holds less than it did before. There is no fixing what is gone. No way to return to the point before it happened. The space stays the same. The work remains, but the result does not. What was expected does not arrive, and nothing steps in to replace it. The process does not slow, does not adjust, does not offer another try. What is lost stays lost, carried forward without comment or pause. This is part of the cost. This life is not gentle. It only looks that way from a distance. Up close, the pressure never lifts. There is no rest hidden inside the stillness, no safety built into the quiet. The absence of sound does not mean the absence of risk. It only hides it better. The surface stays smooth. The struggle stays low. And the weight remains unseen. Even in silence. Effort does not promise an outcome. It only buys a chance. The work can be done right. The timing can hold. Each action only keeps the door open a little longer, holding space for what might happen next. The cost is paid in full before anything is returned. And what comes back is never guaranteed. The system does not reward intent. It only allows a narrow window to stay in play. And sometimes a chance is enough. After everything, some small chances remain. The risk does not leave. The pressure does not ease. Yet movement returns in quiet steps. What once had to be held now starts to push outward, testing the limits that kept it alive. The story shifts toward growth, not because conditions improved, but because something endured long enough to reach this point. Because something survived. The nest grows quiet. No more building. No more fixing. No small returns with new pieces. Movement slows then stops and the space settles into stillness. Nothing changes from one moment to the next. Nothing is added. Nothing is taken away. The shape holds as it is. The balance stays fixed and the air around it feels empty of action. This pause is not choice. It is limit. The work has reached its end and there is nothing left to adjust. What remains now is waiting. Held in place by silence and time because nothing more can be done. The wings stretch out, then rest, then stretch again. Small movements repeated without clear progress. Each motion looks the same as the last. Slow, careful, easy to overlook. Nothing lifts. Nothing out breaks free. And the space around the nest stays unchanged. The body tests its reach, pulls back, holds, then tries once more, adding no distance, gaining no height. From the outside, everything seems still, as if nothing is building toward a change. in diameter and around 3 in deep providing just enough space for the female to lay her eggs and later care for the chicks and constructing a nest typically takes around 5 to 7 Days flamingo nest flamingos are iconic waiting Birds known for their striking pink or red plumage long legs and distinctive curved bills found in shallow coastal regions across the World flamingos are social creatures often seen in large flocks in areas such as Salt Lakes mud flats and Coastal lagoons these birds are famous not only for their beauty but also for their fascinating and unique nesting behaviors which play a crucial role in the survival of their species flamingos build their nests in large colonies typically on mud flats salt pans or Island where there is little disturbance the ideal location is often near water as flamingos rely on aquatic environments for food such as algae Crustaceans and small fish the construction of a flamingo nest involves both the male and female working together they build a mound-like nest out of mud Clay and other organic materials they collect from their surroundings the birds use their bills to gather mud and then they mold the material into a cone-shaped structure the nests are usually about 12 to 18 in high and around 12 in wide at the base providing just enough space for a single egg to rest on top the top of the mound is often smooth down to create a flat surface where the egg can be safely incubated the flamingo's Nest is designed to keep the egg elevated and insulated protecting it from the harsh environment and from potential predators orange footed scrub fowl Nest the orange footed scrub fowl is a unique and fascinating bird found in the forests and Woodlands of Northern Australia and parts of New Guinea known for their distinctive orange colored feet and large robust bodies these birds are part of a group called megapodes which are famous for their unusual nesting Behavior unlike many other birds the orange footed scrub fowl does not incubate its eggs in the traditional manner but instead relies on environmental heat sources to hatch them the nest of the orange footed scrub fowl is an impressive structure that reflects its adaptive Behavior to the environment these birds choose to build their nests on the forest floor typically in a secluded area where the surrounding vegetation offers protection The Chosen location is often near a natural heat Source such as a sun-warmed patch of Earth or decaying vegetation which helps regulate the temperature of the eggs the site is carefully selected to ensure that the eggs are kept at the right temperature for incubation without the need for direct parental warmth The Nest itself is a large Mound constructed from a variety of materials including leaves Twigs dirt and plant matter the orange footed scrub fowl gathers these materials from the forest floor using its strong feet and Beak to pile them into a large Mound which can reach up to 3 ft in height and several feet in diameter the size of the nest provides sufficient space for several eggs typically around 3 to 5 once the mound is built the female lays her eggs at the base and the maale may add more material to maintain the nest's size and warmth the construction of the nest takes several weeks to complete with the birds continually adding to it throughout the nesting season rosate spoon Bill Nest the ros8 above the ground allowing the birds to keep their nests safe from groundbased predators while being close enough to water for easy access to food The Nest is a large bowl-shaped structure made of Twigs sticks and other plant materials which the spoon bills collect from the surrounding area they use their strong bills to gather the materials and arrange them into a sturdy base the female the common raven corvis korax is a highly intelligent and adaptable bird found across a wide range of habitats from forests and mountains to deserts and urban environments known for its glossy black feathers large size and distinctive call The Raven is a resourceful creature with remarkable problem-solving abilities Ravens choose their nest sites with great care they typically build their nests in tall trees cliff or on Ledges where the nest is well protected and offers a good vantage point of the surrounding area while Ravens are known to be opportunistic in their nest site selection they often choose places that are isolated and relatively free from disturbance in some cases they may even nest in man-made structures such as Bridges or tall buildings showing their adaptability to human environments the construction of a Raven's Nest is a detailed and collaborative process both the male and female participate in building the nest using a wide variety of materials that they gather   10. @elizabethcohen6526 [1 likes]: I posted this in a response but thought you all might like to read it

Yes, birds do have a brain that allows them to think and plan complex behaviors like building nests; their brains, particularly a region called the dorsal ventricular ridge (DVR), have structures similar to the mammalian neocortex, which is associated with higher-level thinking, enabling them to not only instinctively build nests but also adapt their construction based on their environment and experience. 
Key points about bird brains and nest building:
Complex brain structure:
While often called "bird-brained," birds actually have a complex brain structure with dedicated areas for processing information related to building nests, navigation, and other intricate behaviors. 
Learning and adaptation:
Nest building isn't purely instinctual; birds can learn and adapt their nest construction based on their environment and past experiences. 
Cerebellum involvement:
Studies have shown that the cerebellum, a part of the brain associated with motor coordination, is particularly active during nest building. 
Dorsal Ventricular Ridge (DVR):
This brain region is considered crucial for complex cognitive functions in birds, similar to the neocortex of mammals.

- Palestine Sunbird: A Stamp of Defiance | Al Jazeera World: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] a [Music] The Palestinian sunbird is a small bird. When I looked it up, I found that even Palestinians give it names based on its appearance and its movements, like the one they call the hummingbird because of its speed, or the one they call Abu Zrik because its wings are blue. The name is nice because it's documented in international bird dictionaries as the Palestinian sunbird, so this gives it a high symbolic value. It adds a lot, and the bird is also very beautiful. It feeds on flower nectar, especially jasmine, which is very common there. I'm sorry to bother you, I'm looking for this bird. I want to see it. If you see it in this area, yes, yes, this is Abu Zrik. It's called the Palestinian sunbird. Yes, we call it Abu Zrik. It's here, here, very common, and it's been around for a long time. I wanted something to express freedom, so the bird is a symbol of freedom. The movement means that the bird can fly, it can reach its destination, and it doesn't need anything. I mean, even in the morning, I noticed that all the birds were flying behind this wall. I felt so lucky they were getting there because the sea isn't far from here, only about 7 kilometers away. When I managed to organize the first exhibition, which was called "At the Checkpoint" (I took its name from the checkpoint itself, since the exhibition was held there), I took pictures of Palestinians who suffered at the Israeli occupation checkpoints. At that time, there were more than 600 checkpoints in the West Bank. Then I decided to do something more impactful by displaying these pictures at the Israeli checkpoint itself, so that the Palestinians there could see their reality and breathe a sigh of relief through the exhibition. The Israelis themselves could also see their own shortcomings, see themselves in this negative image they created for themselves through killing. Of course, my goal was to attract people who would be more interested in the exhibition, to bring them to see the checkpoint, because the exhibition didn't even represent 1% of the suffering of the people at the checkpoint. But when the exhibition attracted people There were foreigners, diplomats, journalists, and even Jews themselves who came to this exhibition. They were drawn to it by its power and its impact on the place. They saw the place in detail; they saw the exhibition for a few minutes, but they saw the barrier for four hours. Of course, the government of Palestine was under British mandate, meaning the British Mandate was, of course, a trusteeship over Palestine from 1918. But there were no administrative or actual institutions in 1918. After 1921, of course, by decision of the High Commissioner, institutions were established— governmental administrative institutions through which the Palestinian seal appeared. The name of the State of Palestine was actually present on documents. Unfortunately, all this historical period was marginalized with the entry of the occupation in 1948. I see from the archives, the existing archival documents, that even the passports of any Palestinian leaving for any region, even Transjordan, were stamped with the phrase "coming from Palestine." The symbols of the government of Palestine were effectively, significantly, and noticeably eliminated through Postage stamps... Of course, we used to see images of Al- Aqsa Mosque, Bethlehem, Jaffa, or Nazareth on postage stamps in ordinary documents and administrative transactions. These were symbols of historical Palestinian cities. Unfortunately, after 1949, all these symbols were erased and replaced with images of Zionist figures—men, figures, and media personalities who worked for the Zionist Agency, like Rimon and Begin, and many others. But today, we are trying to revive these symbols in some way. Unfortunately, I'm trying to imagine... there's no doubt that a process of beautification is taking place in the minds of young people, especially the younger generation—a kind of romantic, dreamy idealism. I was struck by the work of a man I greatly admire, Khaled Jarrar. I saw his work on the Palestinian government stamp. He's trying to restore everything that was destroyed before and after 1948, and he's striving to bring back these symbols to our passports—a beautiful, historical, and modern image of Palestine. [Music] The stamp, which I started working on, was part of a project called "How to Get a Residency Card in Palestine." It was designed to grant a person the right to live and work in Palestine. I created it in a gallery called "Al-Mahatta Gallery" in Ramallah. People would come to the gallery and fill out applications to get this card. It was a performance piece, meant to draw attention to the fact that Palestinians don't have the ability to obtain residency permits or entry visas to the occupied Palestinian territories. This is because the Israeli occupation is the one that grants and determines these things through visas for foreigners. My inspiration came from foreign friends of mine, Nathan and Louise, who lived here and faced the problem of not being able to stay because they needed permission from the occupation. Around March, I started thinking about how to develop the project. I began creating new designs for the stamp until I found the bird, the Palestinian sunbird. I went to the bus station in Ramallah... The guy was there, and that's where I could interact with foreigners. I welcomed them and told them I was a Palestinian artist working on a project called "Where 3H," and that this stamp was the stamp of his Palestinian course, "The Palestinian Sun Duck," and that he wanted to stamp passports with it. They asked what the risk was, and I told them there was definitely a risk. The first person to travel was named Eddie from South Africa, and when they saw his stamp, they said, "We don't know what to say, but it's the first time we've heard that Palestinians have a stamp like this. Maybe Israeli passports were the riskiest." When I met the first girl, she lived in Ramallah and had an American passport. I didn't know she also had an Israeli passport. I stamped her name, Addison, and her American passport. Two months later, she came back and asked me to stamp her Israeli passport. [Laughter] So both from the side of being Jewish and from the side of being American connected to the situation, the conflict here, and so when I Turned 20 i started to feel that i needed to go deeper into what was happening here and as much as i could read i wouldn't understand until i was here myself when i came i discovered that it was incredibly different from what is been portrayed in the media and even what has been ped in many history books especially from the jewish side and colonial and iar more colonial in this context and to see the conflict as i saw before a situation of taking land and expelling people and ethnic cleansing i live in rala i moved into the into i moved out of palestine and into the west b part of pales partially in order to show Israel public that a jewish woman with an israel id can live safely and happily in fact among palestinians um and to end this stigma and this thinking that i would be killed as people told me or that i would be kidnapped so i used my living and my working as a way to constantly be demonstrating against israel bucy and occup yeah well as kh invited me to my passport i was very excited at that At that time, neither Americans nor Israelis had stamped their passports, but the biggest problem that happened to me was when I was at the airport. I was coming back from the United States and I wanted to re-enter at the Israeli airport to Palestine. The manager came to me and said, " Put this in your passport." I said, "This is the stamp of Palestine; it's the Palestinian visa." He said, "There is no such place as Palestine." I said, "What you didn't know is that Palestine was a state before Israel." At that moment, he took me to the interrogation room, and I spent several hours there. In the end, my passport was canceled. postage stamp I made is a Palestinian postage stamp. I printed it here in Ramallah at the beginning so that it could be placed on the applications that people fill out to get temporary residency cards. About a year ago, when I started to complete the stamping process, I made the stamp. When I had an invitation from Art Fair in Paris to exhibit my work there, I contacted the people I wanted to work with and asked them to With the budget in mind, I created the design, prepared it, and sent it to him. We submitted the application to the French post office to display it in the exhibition, but it was rejected. After that, we created what is known as the French postage stamp, which we printed in a printing press—the gallery printed it in a printing press. It was an unofficial stamp. At that time, I was reading the news about the Palestinian prisoner, Hanan Shalabi, who was under administrative detention and had begun a hunger strike. When I was there, she had been on hunger strike for 36 days. I decided to do something because the French newspapers weren't writing about her; no one was talking about her—a person who was dying, no one was mourning her story. So I thought I had to do something to draw attention to her case, to what was happening to her, that she could die at any moment. While I was in Paris, I searched and found a square called Place de la République with a statue of a French woman representing the French Revolution. From there, I found the point of comparison between The Palestinian and French girl, their relationship, and how the revolution for freedom was a powerful message—that's why I decided to change the name of the square from Place d'Or Public (now Shalabi Place) to Shalabi Square. After that, I printed a flyer with a short graphic about Hana, her story, and what was happening to her. I invited people and the press to come to the square on the specified day. There were Palestinians, French people, and journalists. People started coming to show their solidarity, even those who didn't know about Hana's case. They came after hearing about her from others and stood in solidarity. That was the goal. Then the French police arrived and surrounded the square. They tried to threaten me, telling me I had to remove the poster or they'd bring in more police and have me arrested. It was n't the police, it was traffic police. The French and friends who had come the next day gathered around the police. They started speaking to him in French and gave me space to leave. I left, and the sign stayed stuck there. [Music] Welcome. After that, I went to France and presented my work, and it was successful. During this time, I was also invited to exhibit in Berlin, so I contacted them. I sent them the materials I had, the stamp, and the photos documenting the work. I kept in touch with them, constantly corresponding to develop the project further. I asked them to submit it to the German post office to obtain an official German postage stamp. I started my collaboration in August 201, responsible for artic production about utopia, having a passport, how to connect, politics, and the Deutsche Post project. [Music] Toce Pales. A whole year and I carried the stamp with me and the stamp became a part of me, I don't leave it anywhere when I travel, when I go anywhere. This question always came to me, that when will I get my passport stamped and be the first Palestinian passport to be stamped with this stamp? I also had this fear that if I get my passport stamped, will I be able to travel? Will the Israelis prevent me from traveling? The last thing I thought about was getting my passport stamped at that moment, that moment that happened, a very important moment because I didn't want to be prevented from traveling. I wanted to come to Abileen, I wanted to be able to attend this event, and I had to make this line so we could see that my dream had to come true, and the biggest proof is the wall here after 20 years in Berlin, the wall, the day will come when the Israeli wall will collapse. [Applause] What happened was the complete opposite of what I expected. I was expecting them to stop me or overpower me, so when I got it stamped at Checkpoint Charlie in Berlin, I had this feeling, a mixture of fear and hesitation, because I wanted to see how the Israelis would react to the Palestinian passport. What happened was that the soldier there opened the passport, saw their stamp, the Israeli stamp, saw my Palestinian stamp next to it, then he saw the stamps next to his, the tourist stamps they stamped at Checkpoint Charlie. It seems that the stamps, because they are colored green, were originally ordered to be printed at the Belgian post office and were approved. Official stamps were issued by the Belgian post office for the State of Palestine and the Palestinian sunbird. And now I have received a postcard from Belgium with Belgian stamps, in addition to the postcards that arrived from Germany with stamps.
