- What is a Digital Twin?: Kind: captions Language: en A digital twin is a virtual representation of an object or a system. Like me! A digital twins are updated with real time data which can be used for machine learning and reasoning to make better decisions. Disturbing, but yes. And what can we do with a digital twin? Let's get into it. Well, that was odd. Digital twins are usually objects like wind turbines, and they're fitted with various sensors. And those sensors capture data and different aspects of the physical object performance, like in this case, energy output and temperature. And that live data is applied to the digital copy of the wind turbine, its digital twin. From there, we can study all sorts of potential situations, analyze performance problems and create potential enhancements that can be used to improve the real life physical object. Now, doesn't this all sound a little bit like a simulation? Kind of does. But digital twins are actually virtual environments that operate quite differently from simulations. Now, in a simulation, we model a system or a process to understand its behavior under specific conditions and simulations, they are static snapshots in time. They are snapshots in time that represent predefined scenarios. But a digital twin, it goes beyond this. It's a living representation of a specific physical asset. So unlike a simulation, a digital twin is continuously updated using real time data. That's data from sensors reflecting its unique state. So, for example, a digital twin of a turbine in a wind farm can track its real time blade vibration and temperature. Predicting maintenance needs and optimizing performance. And digital twins are designed around a two way flow of information that occurs when the object sensors provide relevant data to the digital twin. and then that happens again when the insights created by the digital twin are shared back with the original source object. Well, simulations tell us how things should work. Digital twins show us how things are working, right now. Now there are several types of digital twin depending upon what you want to model. Let's take a look at them. And the first one is a component twin. A component twins are the basic unit of a digital twin. They represent individual components or parts of a system. Capturing their specific behavior and performance, a component twin could model the behavior of a single blade on a wind turbine. Tracking its stress levels, vibration patterns and weather over time. Next layer up is asset twins. Now, asset twins represent an entire asset made up of multiple components working together, and asset twins let you study the interaction of those components, creating a bunch of performance data that can be processed and then turned into actionable insights. An asset twin for a wind turbine would integrate data from all of its components, like the blades and the gearbox and the generator to monitor overall performance and predict failures. Even broader, we have system twins and system twins enable you to see how different assets come together to form an entire functioning system. A system twin could model an entire wind farm, tracking how multiple turbines interact with each other to optimize energy output. And then at the macro level, we have process twins, and process twins reveal how systems work together to create an entire production facility. Process twins can identify inefficiencies and optimize processes across multiple systems. A process twin for wind energy might model how energy is generated, stored and distributed from a wind farm to the power grids. Considering factors like energy demand and storage capacity. So we've talked a lot about wind turbines, and there's a reason for that, and that is because things like power generation equipment are fantastic use cases for digital twins, large engines like jet engines, locomotive engines and, yes, turbines. They can benefit tremendously from the use of digital twins, especially for helping to establish time frames for regularly needed maintenance, but where else are they used? Well, another good use case is in big physical structures like large buildings or offshore drilling platforms. Digital twins can be useful in designing the systems within those structures. So things like hvac systems. And since digital twins are meant to mirror a product's entire lifecycle, it's not surprising that digital twins have become ubiquitous in all stages of manufacturing. Getting products from the design stage to the finished product and all the steps in between. And then another common use case is urban planning as well. This is where civil engineers can use digital twins to show 3D and 4D these spatial data in real time, and also incorporate augmented reality systems into these built environments. Look, the term digital twin was credited to NASA's John Vickers back in 2010, but digital twins are constantly learning new skills and capabilities, meaning they can continue to generate the insights needed to make products better and processes more efficient. Be that wind turbines or creepy digital versions of YouTube presenters.

And even an owl that decided mice were boring and went fishing instead. Today, we're diving into a world most travelers never see. 15 strange bird species of Laos you won't believe exist. From the legendary giant ibis, a prehistoricl looking giant that's one of the rarest birds on earth to the jewelike pas hiding in the shadows to the neon sparkling forktailed sunbird that could outshine a rainbow. The flashy diva, the mysterious shadow, or the ancient survivor? Let's find out. Number 15, the green peo. Here's the hook. What bird looks like it rolled straight out of a royal parade, but is actually one of Southeast Asia's most endangered treasures? That's the green peel. Forget everything you know about the Indian blue poul, the one that struts around Palace Gardens. This is its rarer, wilder cousin, living in the forests and wetlands of Laos. And trust me, when the sunlight hits those feathers, it's like the jungle suddenly switched on neon mode. The look absolutely dazzling. Males are covered in metallic green and gold scales across the body. A fan-shaped train with shimmering eye spots and a tall crest like a crown. Even the females are gorgeous. Unlike their blue pile relatives, green pee hens keep much of the same shimmering plumage, just slightly toned down. This means both sexes walk the forest like royalty. Behavior- wise, they're ground dwellers, strutting with long legs through tall grass or along riversides. And when a male decides to display, boom, the tail goes up, feathers shimmer, and the whole bird transforms into a walking firework. diet. Omnivores through and through. Fruits, seeds, insects, even small reptiles. If it fits in the beak, it's on the menu. Fun fact, in ancient Southeast Asian cultures, the green pea foul was a symbol of beauty, prosperity, and protection. Kings considered them sacred. Seeing one in the wild today feels like stumbling into a living legend. Mini twist. Despite their glamour, these birds are in trouble. Habitat loss and hunting have pushed them onto the endangered list. In Laos, their populations are tiny and fragmented, making every sighting a rare and unforgettable moment. And the call, let's just say subtle isn't their style. Loud, piercing cries echo across the forest, announcing their presence like royal trumpets. So, here's my question for you. I want to see how many of you are brave enough to strut like a poul. Number 14, the spotted elakura. Here's a question. What if I told you there's a bird so unique that it doesn't just get its own species, it gets its own entire family tree? That's the spotted elakura. This tiny secretive bird lives in the dense mountain forests of Laos and nearby countries. And when I say tiny, I mean sparrow size. But don't let the size fool you. Scientists discovered that genetically it's not closely related to any other small bird in Asia. In fact, it was placed into its own family in 2014. Talk about being the ultimate black sheep. Or maybe the black and white sheep. The look, brown body covered in neat white spots and streaks with a short tail that flicks nervously as it creeps through undergrowth. Nothing too flashy until you hear it. It song is a loud musical series of whistles that echo through the forest like a flute player practicing deep in the jungle. Diet, insects, spiders, and little invertebrates. The spotted elakura hops around near the forest floor, staying low and hidden while it forages. Blink and you'll miss it. Fun fact, this bird is so shy that for decades, many bird watchers thought it was just another plain old renabler. It took genetic analysis to finally reveal, nope, this is something completely different. A living relic of a very old evolutionary branch. Many twist because it's so hard to see. Spotting a spotted elashure in Laos is like hitting the bird watchers jackpot. Most people will hear its haunting whistle long before they ever lay eyes on it. So, here's my question for you. Would you rather be a bird that looks plain but has a voice that stops people in their tracks, or one that's dazzling to the eye but completely silent? Drop your answer in the comments. I'm curious which kind of strange bird you'd rather be. Number 13, the giant ibis. Here's a hook for you. What's bigger than a stork, rarer than a unicorn, and basically the holy grail for bird watchers in Southeast Asia? That's the giant ibis. This bird isn't just strange, it's a legend. Standing over a meter tall with a wingspan close to 7 ft, it is the largest ibis species on Earth. Imagine a heron crossed with a prehistoric dinosaur and you're getting close. The look, a massive gray brown body, long legs built for waiting, and a long down curved bill that looks like it could open cans of soup. Add the bald, scaly head, and you've got a bird that feels more Jurassic Park than Jungle Temple. But here's the twist. Despite its size, the giant ibis is incredibly hard to find. It lives mostly in remote wetlands and lowland forests of southern Laos and Cambodia. places so wild and inaccessible that even scientists struggle to reach them. Diet: frogs, insects, crustaceians, even small reptiles. If it moves in the mud, the giant ibis probably eats it. They probe their big bills deep into soft soil, pulling up whatever unlucky creature is hiding below. Fun fact, in Cambodian folklore, the giant ibis is considered a spirit bird, an omen of wilderness itself. And for burers, seeing one in the wild is a once-in-a-lifetime achievement. No exaggeration, many twist. Sadly, this bird is critically endangered. With fewer than 300 left in the world, its survival hangs by a thread. Deforestation, hunting, and drained wetlands are pushing it to the edge. Which means every single sighting isn't just exciting, it's history in the making. So, here's my question for you. If you had the chance to see one of the world's rarest birds in the wild, would you keep the location secret to protect it or tell everyone so they could share the wonder? Drop your answer in the comments. I'd love to hear where you stand on that one. Number 12, the white rumped falcon. Here's the hook. What raptor looks cute enough to fit in your palm, but hunts like a tiny assassin of the skies? That's the white rumped falcon. At just about the size of a pigeon, this is one of the smallest falcons in the world. Found in Laos and a few neighboring countries, it's the ultimate case of, don't judge by size. Small frame, huge attitude. Visually, it's striking. Snow white head and underparts, dark wings and back, and that namesake bright white rump flashing when it flies. Add a sharp curved beak and fiery eyes, and you realize this isn't some backyard bird. It's a pint-sized predator. Behavior. Classic falcon tactics, just miniaturized. They perch quietly on exposed branches, scanning the area like snipers. When prey comes into range, wham! They swoop down at breakneck speed, snatching insects, small lizards, or even tiny birds right out of the air. Blink and you'll miss the strike. Fun fact, because they're so small and elusive, white rumped falcons are rarely seen. For bird watchers in Laos, spotting one perched on a dead branch in the open is like striking gold. It's one of those moments you brag about for years. Many twist. Don't let that adorable size fool you. They're fierce. Locals sometimes call them little tigers of the sky because of the way they fearlessly take on prey almost their own size. Imagine a bird that could fit in your hand taking down another bird mid-flight. That's hardcore diet. Mostly large insects like grasshoppers, beetles, and cicas. But they're opportunistic. If something smaller and slower crosses their path, it's on the menu. And here's the kicker. Unlike many other falcons that live in wide open plains, this species prefers forest edges and bamboo groves. It's not about speed over miles. It's about surgical strikes in tight spaces. So, here's my question for you. Would you rather be big and powerful but expected to win or small and underestimated than shocking everyone when you do? Tell me in the comments. I'm curious how many of you would choose the underdog life just like the white rumped falcon. Number 11, the blackared shrike babbler. Here's a question. What bird looks like it got dressed by mixing and matching outfits from three totally different species? That's the blackire shrike babbler. This bird has puzzled scientists and bird watchers for years. Partly because it doesn't fit neatly into any one group. It's got the hooked bill of a shrike, the colors of a tropical songird, and the social behavior of a babbler. Basically, it's the Frankenstein of the bird world, but in a good way. Visually, it's a stunner. olive green back, white belly, black mask around the face, and that trademark black ear patch outlined in white. Males sometimes show bright chestnut or yellow touches depending on the subspecies. It's the kind of bird that makes you do a double take and go, "Wait, what am I looking at?" In Laos, you'll find it in montaine forest moving through the canopy and mixed flocks. And trust me, it does not travel alone. These birds are social butterflies, constantly calling, chattering, and joining up with other species like they're at an all-day jungle block party. Diet: Insects are the main course, but they'll also snack on berries. With that hooked bill, they can snatch bugs right off leaves or branches with surprising precision. Fun fact, for years, ornithologists argued about where this bird belonged on the evolutionary tree. Shrike, babbler, or something else entirely. Turns out genetic studies placed it closer to viros, a whole different family. Translation: The bird fooled experts for decades. Mini twist. While its name sounds like it belongs in a horror movie. Blackared Shrike Babbler. The bird itself is actually pretty cheerful, constantly singing and calling as it forages. It's like the jungle DJ adding background music wherever it goes. So, rather be a bird that confuses everyone with your identity or one that's simple and straightforward? Tell me in the comments. I think I'd lean toward the mystery vibe like the Shrike Babbler. Number 10, the white-winged duck. Here's the hook. What duck spends its life hiding in the shadows of jungle swamps, but when it finally takes flight, flashes wings so bright they look like lightning? That's the white-winged duck. At first glance, it looks dark and almost plain. A big, heavy-bodied duck with blackish brown plumage, pale speckles on the head, and a chunky bill. But wait until it opens its wings. Boom! Huge white patches blaze against the dark feathers like surprise headlights in the night. It's a secret superpower hidden until takeoff. This duck is one of the largest in Asia, often stretching close to 90 cm long. But unlike those cheerful park ducks waddling around for breadcrumbs, the white-winged duck is elusive, shy, and prefers remote jungle pools and slow flowing streams. In Laos, they lurk in quiet forest wetlands far from human eyes. Diet, a classic swamp mix. seeds, aquatic plants, snails, insects, and the occasional small fish. They feed mostly at dusk or dawn, quietly dabbling or diving in shadowy backwaters. Fun fact, these ducks are sometimes called ghost ducks by locals because you can spend hours in the swamp and never see one. Just ripples in the water or a sudden flash of white wings as they vanish into the trees. Mini twist. While they look tough, the white-winged duck is globally endangered. There may be fewer than 1,000 left in the wild. Habitat loss, wetland drainage, and hunting have pushed them to the edge. Spotting one in Laos isn't just rare. It's like winning the wildlife lottery. And here's the kicker. Because they're so secretive, even scientists don't know everything about them. Each new sighting adds a piece to the puzzle of how these ducks survive in fragmented forests. So, rather be flashy all the time or keep your beauty hidden until the perfect dramatic moment? Drop your answer in the comments. I think the white-winged duck has already mastered the art of mystery. Number nine, the silver feeasant. Here's the hook. What bird looks like it walks straight out of a royal costume ball, but actually prefers strutting through mountain forests? That's the silver feeasant. Native to Southeast Asia, including parts of Laos, this pheasant is pure elegance. The male is breathtaking. Snow white feathers etched with fine black lines, long flowing tail, glossy black underparts, and a bright crimson face that looks like a mask. The female, more camouflaged in brown, but still sleek and graceful. Together, they're the jungle's version of a power couple. When the male walks, it's pure theater. tail feathers trailing, red face glowing, crest lifted high. It looks like the forest rolled out a runway just for him. And during breeding season, he takes it up a notch, spreading his tail, bowing, and calling loudly to impress the ladies. Think tuxedo, spotlight, and a very dramatic performance. Diet, omnivores by nature. Seeds, berries, shoots, and insects are all on the menu. They spend much of their time scratching through leaf litter, foraging like feathered gourmes of the forest floor. Fun fact, the silver feeasant has been admired for centuries. In ancient Chinese art and royal symbolism, it often represented beauty, power, and integrity. Today, it's still kept in aviaries worldwide for its striking appearance. But seeing one in the wild, free in the forests of Laos, is a completely different experience. mini twist. While they look untouchable in their regal costumes, wild silver feeasants face threats from hunting and habitat loss. Their beauty, ironically, puts them at risk. And here's something quirky. Despite their elegance, males can be feisty. They'll defend territory with loud calls and even charge intruders, proving that style and toughness can go hand in hand. So, here's my question for you. Would you rather be remembered for your strength or your style? Tell me in the comments. I think the silver feeasant just proved you can be both. Number eight, the blue rumped pitha. Here's the hook. What bird is so shy you almost never see it. Yet when it finally steps into the light, it looks like it's dressed for a jungle carnival. That's the blue rumped pa. This secretive bird spends most of its time in the dense, damp forests of Laos, sneaking around the understory where light barely reaches. For hours, all you might hear is a low, haunting whistle. And then, if you're lucky, it hops into view, and suddenly, the forest floor explodes with color. The male has rich chestnut plumage, a bright golden crown, and of course, that dazzling turquoise blue rum that flashes like neon every time it flicks its wings. The female is more subdued, olive brown with hints of blue, but still gorgeous in her own earthy way. Diet, earthworms, insects, and snails. They forage quietly on the forest floor, probing the leaf litter like little jewel hunters. Watching a peta feed feels like nature's version of hideand-seek. They vanish, then reappear when you least expect it. Fun fact, birders often call pas the jewels of the forest. And the blue rumped pa lives up to that reputation. It's basically a gemstone with feathers. Many twist. Despite being so stunning, most locals hear them far more often than they ever see them. These birds are masters of staying hidden. In fact, many photographers spend days camped out in the forest just for a few seconds of a blue rumped pa stepping into frame. And here's the kicker. When they do show up, it feels magical. A flash of blue and gold in the shadows and then gone. It's like the forest gave you a gift and snatched it back before you could say thank you. So, here's my question for you. Would you rather be a bird that everyone sees all the time or one that stays hidden but blows minds every time you appear? Drop your answer in the comments. I have a feeling this PETA would definitely vote for mystery. Number seven, the Mountain Imperial Pigeon. Here's the hook. What pigeon is so big and majestic that it makes your average city pigeon look like a scrappy street punk? That's the mountain imperial pigeon. This giant of the pigeon world is the largest pigeon species in Southeast Asia. In Lios's forest, when one glides overhead, it looks less like a dove and more like a small hawk. Seriously, city pigeons would ask for its autograph if they could. Visually, it's elegant, not flashy. A smooth blend of soft gray and brown plumage, pale underparts, and a faint purplish gloss on the neck that shines in the right light. Add those calm, dark eyes, and you've got a bird that radiates quiet authority. Behavior- wise, these pigeons are canopy dwellers. They love tall evergreen forests, perching high above the ground, where they can watch everything below. And when they take flight, their powerful wing beats echo through the trees, announcing their presence like thunder. Diet, mostly fruits and berries. They swallow fruits whole, pits and all, and then fly off to spread seeds across the forest. Translation: They're not just big, they're critical gardeners of the jungle. Fun fact, despite their massive size, mountain imperial pigeons are surprisingly graceful in flight. Watching one soar across a valley is like watching a feathered glider plane. No flapping frenzy, just smooth, effortless power. Mini twist. Their call isn't a cheerful coup. It's a deep booming ho ooh that resonates through the forest like a fog horn. If you're camping in Laos and hear it at dawn, don't worry. You're not being haunted. It's just this imperial giant greeting the morning. And here's the kicker. While they're not globally endangered, they are vulnerable to hunting and habitat loss. In some regions, populations are shrinking fast, reminding us that even common giants can disappear if we don't protect their homes. So, here's my question for you. Would you rather be flashy and showy or calm and powerful like the mountain imperial pigeon? Drop your answer in the comments. I think a lot of us could use a little of that pigeon confidence. Number six, the black breasted thrush. Here's the hook. What bird shows up to the forest dressed like it's heading to a blacktai gala but sings like it's auditioning for the voice? That's the black breasted thrush. Native to the mountains of Laos and neighboring countries, this thrush is elegance on wings. The male has a glossy black head and chest, bright orange bill, and snowy white underparts that look like a tuxedo. The female more low-key, warm brown tone, softer, but still graceful. Behavior- wise, these thrushes are classic understory dwellers. They forage on the forest floor, hopping among leaf litter for insects, snails, and berries. Watch one for a few minutes, and you'll see that stop, hop, pause like they're dancing to their own invisible beat. And the song, pure magic. Males sing rich, melodious notes at dawn and dusk, filling misty mountain forest with music that makes you stop in your tracks. If you didn't know better, you'd think you were listening to a professional flute player practicing deep in the woods. Fun fact, burers in Laos often say you'll hear the black breasted thrush long before you see it. They're shy, quick to slip into shadows. Spotting one is like finding the headliner after listening to the sound check all morning. Mini twist. Despite their elegance, these birds aren't invincible. Deforestation and hunting pressure in parts of Southeast Asia threaten their numbers, which means every encounter is a gift and a reminder that even the most beautiful songs can go silent if we're not careful. And here's something quirky. During courtship, the male sometimes bows and fluffs his feathers like a little opera singer hitting a dramatic note. Imagine a tuxedo tenor serenating his partner under the trees. So, here's my question for you. Would you rather have a voice so good the whole forest stops to listen or a look so sharp you never need an introduction? Tell me in the comments. I want to know which superpower you'd pick. Number five, the forktailed sunbird. Here's the hook. What bird is smaller than your phone but shines brighter than a jewelry store window? That's the forktailed sunbird. This tiny nectar lover is pure sparkle in the forest of Laos. The male is a living rainbow. Metallic green head, crimson throat and chest, golden yellow belly, and that trademark forked tail flashing behind him. The female keeps it subtle with olive green and yellow tones. But together, they're the jungle's power couple. Behavior- wise, these birds are the hummingbirds of Asia. Quick, restless, always darting from flower to flower, they use their curved bills to sip nectar, powering that non-stop energy. And just like hummingbirds, they don't mind chasing each other or even bigger birds away from their favorite flowers. Small bird, big attitude. Diet, mostly nectar, but they'll snap up insects, too, especially during breeding season. Think of them as multitaskers. Part pollinator, part pest control, all wrapped up in glittery feathers. Fun fact, the forktailed sunbird is a master of suncatching. When sunlight hits at just the right angle, the male's feathers blaze with iridescent colors that look almost unreal. It's like someone cranked up the saturation filter on nature. Mini twist. Despite their beauty, they're tiny, barely 10 cm long, but their song is surprisingly strong. a cheerful series of whistles and warbles that carry through the forest like a soundtrack to their flower hopping ballet. And here's the kicker. These birds play a critical role in pollination. Without them, many jungle flowers wouldn't reproduce as well. So yeah, they're not just eye candy, they're ecological VIPs. So here's my question for you. If you could glow in one color every time sunlight hit you, what would it be? Drop your answer in the comments. I'm betting a few of you are going to choose rainbow mode, just like this forktailed sunbird. Number four, the bar-bellied pitha. Here's the hook. What bird looks like it escaped from an art studio, wearing brush strokes of every bright color the painter had left on the pallet? That's the barb-ied pa. This secretive ground dweller lives in Lao's dense, humid forest. And trust me, when you finally catch a glimpse, it's like the forest floor suddenly turned into a parade float. The male is a kaleidoscope. Brilliant green back, cobalt blue wings, fiery orange belly, and those bold yellow and black bars across the chest that give it its name. The female, slightly less flashy, but still rocking emerald green and golden tones. Honestly, she'd outshine half the parrots at a zoo. Behavior-wise, pas are shy. They prefer hopping quietly on the ground, turning leaves to find insects, snails, and worms. But when startled, they can burst into flight, flashing colors like a magic trick before vanishing into the understory again. Fun fact, bird watchers call pitas the jewel birds of Asia, and the bar-bellied pa might be one of the brightest jewels of them all. But here's the twist. You could spend days in its forest and never see it. They're masters of hiding, most people only hear their distinctive whistles echoing through the trees. Mini twist. Despite their beauty, bar-bellied pas are vulnerable to habitat loss. Deforestation across Southeast Asia is chipping away at their homes, which means every rare sighting is not just breathtaking, it's bittersweet. And here's something quirky. During breeding season, the male sometimes performs little hops and wing flicks as a courtship dance. Imagine a jewel bird trying to impress with a goofy two-step. you rather be dazzling but hard to find or common but overlooked? Drop your answer in the comments. I have a feeling a few of you are going to side with this hidden gem of a bird. Number three, the blackheaded woodpecker. Here's the hook. What bird shows up in the forest looking like it dipped its head in ink, then threw on a neon yellow jacket just to confuse you? That's the blackheaded woodpecker. This striking bird is found in Laos, Vietnam, in parts of Southeast Asia. And let me tell you, it's impossible to mistake for anything else. The male sports a jet black head, bright greenish yellow back, and wings with electric blue highlights. Add a red crown if you're a male, and boom. You've got a bird that looks like it was designed by a street artist with too many spray cans. Behavior-wise, it's a classic woodpecker drumming on trunks, probing for insects, and chiseling into deadwood. But here's the fun part. Unlike some woodpeckers that stick to thick forests, this species is happy in open woodlands and even semi-arid zones. Translation: It's adaptable, tough, and just a little unpredictable. diet. Mostly ants and termites. These guys can tear open entire nests with that powerful beak, lapping up insects like a kid with a bowl of popcorn. Fun fact, their calls are a sharp laughing cackle, something between a parrot and a joker. If you're walking through the forest and hear laughter that sounds a little unhinged, it might just be this bird mocking you. Mini twist. Despite looking flamboyant, the blackheaded woodpecker is not as rare as some of Laous's other species. But here's the catch. Because they're bold and noisy, they're often the first woodpecker species hunters target. Flashiness has its risks. And here's the kicker. Watching one hammer into a termite mound is like watching a feather jackhammer at work. Chips fly, dust scatters, and within minutes, the bird's belly is full. Efficient, destructive, and kind of impressive. So, here's my question for you. Would you rather stand out like a neon sign and risk being noticed or blend in quietly and play it safe? Drop your answer in the comments. I think the blackheaded woodpecker already made its choice. Number two, the air pa. Here's the hook. What bird looks like it borrowed a costume from an ancient warrior but spends most of its life sneaking quietly in the shadows? That's the irred pa. This rare forest bird of Laos is part of the jewelike pa family, but it takes a very different approach to fashion. Instead of neon colors, the irid pa wears earthy tones, rich browns, olive greens, and black, all speckled with intricate white markings. And then there are the ears. Long pale streaks running down the sides of the head that look like ribbons flowing from a helmet. It's the kind of bird that blends perfectly into the forest floor, vanishing among leaves and shadows. Blink and you'll swear it was never there. Diet, worms, insects, and snails. Like other pas, it forages on the ground, probing leaf litter with that strong bill. Watching one feed is like watching a jewel thief tiptoe through the dark, lifting treasures only it can see. Fun fact, the eared pitha is notoriously hard to find. Even experienced bird watchers can spend days in the forest and come back empty-handed. Hearing their soft, mournful whistles in the distance is often the only proof they're around. Many twist. Despite being shy, the eared pita plays an important role in the ecosystem, helping to control insect populations and recycling nutrients on the forest floor. Quiet, hidden, but essential. And here's something fascinating. Unlike its showier cousins, the aid pa relies on subtle beauty. Up close, those markings are unbelievably detailed, like fine embroidery stitched feather by feather. It's nature's camouflage couture. So, here's my question for you. Would you rather shine bright like a neon superstar or keep things subtle but classy like the eared pa? Tell me in the comments. I think this bird proves that sometimes low-key is the most stylish of all. Number one, the tawny fish owl. Here's the hook. What owl swapped spooky midnight hunts in the forest for fishing trips by the river? That's the tawny fish owl. This isn't your typical ghostly night hunter. Instead of silently swooping through dark woods for mice, this owl prefers river banks and mountain streams where it specializes in, yep, catching fish. Picture a big orange brown owl perched on a branch, staring down into the water like a grumpy old fisherman waiting for a bite. The look impressive. Tawny brown feathers stre with darker bars, long ear toughs sticking up like wild eyebrows and piercing yellow eyes that glow like lanterns. Add powerful featherless legs ending in massive talons, and you've got a bird built for grabbing slippery prey straight out of the water. diet. Mostly fish, but it's not picky. Frogs, crabs, even the occasional rodent make the menu. If it moves near water, this owl is interested. Fun fact, unlike many owls, the tawny fish owl isn't fully nocturnal. It often hunts at dusk or even during the day, especially around shaded streams. Imagine stumbling across an owl fishing while you're on a hike. It's equal parts bizarre and awesome. Mini twist. Despite its power, this owl can be surprisingly clumsy on the ground. Watching one waddle along a riverbank feels less like a top predator and more like Bigfoot on a fishing trip. But the second it launches into the air, you're reminded it's still very much the king of the stream. And here's the kicker. This species is rare in Laos because clear, unpolluted rivers are disappearing fast. Protecting their watery hunting grounds means protecting much more than just one bird. So, here's my question for you. If you were an owl, would you stick to the classic mouse diet or switch it up and go fishing like this tawny fish owl? Drop your answer in the comments. - Business Development Manager Career Path, Skills, and Responsibilities | What is a BDM?: Kind: captions Language: en Curious about what a Business Development&nbsp; Manager is? I'm Bryan Rios from Wahl+Case and&nbsp;&nbsp; in this video you will learn everything you need&nbsp; to know about being a Business Development Manager&nbsp;&nbsp; in SaaS and IT. What it is, What they do, and&nbsp; the skills you need to land a job as a BDM. At&nbsp;&nbsp; Wahl+Case, we've helped hundreds of people&nbsp; land Business Development Manager jobs at&nbsp;&nbsp; some of the hottest tech companies in adtech,&nbsp; fintech, consumer tech, and enterprise tech,&nbsp;&nbsp; so keep watching if you want to learn all about&nbsp; business development. Before we get started,&nbsp;&nbsp; make sure you're subscribed to our youtube&nbsp; channel for even more market explainers,&nbsp;&nbsp; industry insights, and tech trends from Japan.&nbsp; Okay, so what is a Business Development Manager?&nbsp;&nbsp; First, I think it'll help to define business&nbsp; development. BD or Business Development is&nbsp;&nbsp; the process of bringing in new customers or new&nbsp; business lines to help a business grow. Like the&nbsp;&nbsp; name says, you are developing business. This could&nbsp; mean that you need to cold call or email prospects&nbsp;&nbsp; converting them into leads and then nurturing&nbsp; those leads to make sure they buy your solution&nbsp;&nbsp; or, if you are working at a larger company, you&nbsp; could be researching new trends in tech that your&nbsp;&nbsp; company will want to acquire or invest in as a&nbsp; way to bring in another revenue stream. All of&nbsp;&nbsp; these responsibilities fall under the umbrella&nbsp; of business development. Which sounds the most&nbsp;&nbsp; exciting to you? Let us know in the comments.&nbsp; What does a business development manager&nbsp;&nbsp; do? There are two kinds of BDMs: The first&nbsp; one is an expansion on the roles of the BDR&nbsp;&nbsp; which is Business Development Representative&nbsp; building up an existing business line&nbsp;&nbsp; by developing leads into customers and maintaining&nbsp; those relationships. For a business entering a&nbsp;&nbsp; market, this will be the first hire. They will&nbsp; generate customers that will then be passed on&nbsp;&nbsp; to the second hire which is usually a CSM. See our&nbsp; video about Customer Success to learn more about&nbsp;&nbsp; what a CSM does. The big difference between being&nbsp; a Business Development Manager and a BDR is that&nbsp;&nbsp; you will need to close deals and you will handle&nbsp; those negotiations by yourself. You will not pass&nbsp;&nbsp; these leads onto an Account Executive or another&nbsp; sales function. In a large enterprise, however,&nbsp;&nbsp; you would be focused on building the business by&nbsp; developing new business lines. You will need to&nbsp;&nbsp; be following developing trends in technology&nbsp; to find new revenue streams or opportunities&nbsp;&nbsp; for investments or acquisitions. This is the&nbsp; second type of Business Development Manager.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is why in our M&A guide with Fuminori Gunji,&nbsp; he highlights some of his time at Softbank. Side&nbsp;&nbsp; note: if you haven't seen that I highly recommend&nbsp; checking it out, I'll put a card up in the corner.&nbsp;&nbsp; Targets and KPI. As a Business Development Manager&nbsp; your general target is to grow the business.&nbsp;&nbsp; This means that your KPI will be: revenue, deals&nbsp; closed, and client meetings. Again, these could&nbsp;&nbsp; be more focused on partnerships or new revenue&nbsp; streams if you are working at a bigger company&nbsp;&nbsp; but the overall goal is the same: develop the&nbsp; business. So what skills do you need to work&nbsp;&nbsp; in business development? Hard skills: working as&nbsp; a business development manager means connecting&nbsp;&nbsp; with people so there are very few essential&nbsp; hard skills. Of course, it always helps to&nbsp;&nbsp; have a knowledge of the tech you are working with&nbsp; or selling and the industry you're going to be&nbsp;&nbsp; working in to be able to talk through any&nbsp; concerns that your clients have. Soft skills:&nbsp;&nbsp; your communication skills and listening skills&nbsp; will be honed in a BDM role. You will be&nbsp;&nbsp; constantly conversing with your clients to really&nbsp; understand the issues that need to be solved&nbsp;&nbsp; with your solution. You will also be handling&nbsp; sales presentations and contract negotiations,&nbsp;&nbsp; so your communication abilities will definitely&nbsp; grow. For me, when I was working in a sales role,&nbsp;&nbsp; business development was my favorite part. I&nbsp; really enjoyed meeting with clients and listening&nbsp;&nbsp; to what was happening with their business and&nbsp; connecting over a shared passion for the industry.&nbsp;&nbsp; There really is nothing quite like the feeling&nbsp; of becoming part of a team to solve a particular&nbsp;&nbsp; problem. Does this sound exciting to you? Message&nbsp; us in the link in the description to learn what&nbsp;&nbsp; business development positions are open right now.&nbsp; Career Progression. A BDM is an essential function&nbsp;&nbsp; of any sales organization. When you develop this&nbsp; skill set, you can move in almost any direction&nbsp;&nbsp; in sales. Working as a business development&nbsp; manager can also open doors for you to take&nbsp;&nbsp; on leadership positions. We have seen many people&nbsp; move into Country Manager or Director level roles&nbsp;&nbsp; after spending time as a Business Development&nbsp; Manager. If your long-term goal is to hold a&nbsp;&nbsp; leadership position, working as a BDM is a great&nbsp; step. And that's it! Did this help you understand&nbsp;&nbsp; what a business development manager does? If it&nbsp; did type BDM in the comments. Remember to like&nbsp;&nbsp; this video if you found it helpful and share&nbsp; it with anyone you think would benefit from&nbsp;&nbsp; learning about business development. Thanks&nbsp; for watching and we'll see you next time!

For the pharaohs building those monuments, however, things were not so simple. Egyptian kings chose their burial places with an eye to the gods, but also with concern for the massive logistical effort involved in building these legendary structures. - Discovering the Majestic National Birds of Southeast Asia: Kind: captions Language: en southeast Asia is a region known for its Rich biodiversity they have chosen these birds to symbolize their unique natural heritage here are the national birds of Southeast Asian countries the national bird of Brunei darussalam is the white-bellied Sea Eagle it has a slender body a white head and Underside while the wings are slate gray or black like other eagles this bird is a carnivore that prefers to hunt down living prey and rarely if ever scavenges already deceased animals the giant Ibis is a huge striking Ibis unique by virtue of its being the largest member of its family as well as being the only member of its genus this species is cambodia's national bird and owing to its Rarity and exceptional size holds near mythical status for bird watchers naturalists and conservationists the Javon hawk eagle or Garuda pankasila in Indonesian is the national bird of Indonesia this Majestic bird of prey is endemic to the islands of java and Bali it is characterized by its striking appearance with a white head and breast Dark Wings and distinctive crest of feathers on its head the Javan Hawke Eagle represents strength and freedom in Indonesian culture the Magnificent green pea fowl is rare endangered and declining overall but still locally common and observable in several protected areas where it is a centerpiece of conservation and ecotourism male is among the most elegant and spectacular of birds turkey-sized tall slender long-legged with a rigid vertical Crest glistening green blue and golden plumage and an extravagant tail that can exceed two meters long Malaysia's national bird the Rhinoceros hornbill is a magnificent and distinctive bird with a large colorful Cask on its bill it is known for its striking black plumage white belly and bright red eye ring and legs this hornbill is revered for its impressive appearance and represents unity and Harmony in Malaysian culture myanmar's national bird is the Burmese pea fowl a close relative of the more well-known Indian peacock the male Burmese pea fowl is known for its striking blue-green plumage and vibrant eye spots on its tail feathers this bird symbolizes Beauty and Grace in myanmar's culture the national bird of the Philippines is the Philippine eagle also known as the monkey eating eagle this critically endangered bird is one of the largest and most powerful eagles in the world is known for its striking appearance with a massive beak and a crest of feathers on its head the Philippine eagle symbolizes the country's strength and commitment to conservation nature lovers and bird watchers in particular were all in a tizzy when the nature Society announced that the Crimson Sunbird was the national bird of Singapore at the 2015 Asian bird fair the male bird is strikingly red with dark gray underparts a dark blue crown and tail as well as dark streaks on its face Siamese fireback is Thailand's bird symbolizes the vibrant and diverse Wildlife found in Thailand timor-leste's national bird is the teamer Imperial pigeon a large gray pigeon with yellow eyes and a dark Bill shows uniform dark gray upper parts and paler gray head and underparts becoming buff on the undertone juveniles display reddish-brown fringes to wing and tail feathers golden winged laughing thrush is The Unofficial - How to Grow Sunflowers Successfully At Home 🌻: Kind: captions Language: en Sunflowers are one of the easiest and sunniest and brightest things that you can add to your garden to get both ornamental and edible value. Kevin Espiritu here from Epic Gardening, where it's my goal to help you grow greener thumb. This thing is almost as. Big as my head. Look at that! Just kidding. It's still half the size of. My head, which makes me really sad about the size of my head. Nevertheless, we are going to talk today about different varieties of sunflower you can grow, different strategies for growing them, where they make sense to grow, all the different conditions of them, all that kind of stuff. So cultivate that Like button and I will bless you with a sunflower bigger than my head, which is hard to do. And let's get into the video. Sunflowers are native to North America, which is pretty cool. We have some plants that are really popular, that are native here, that have kind of made it all around the world. Dry beans, corn, things like that. Indigenous Americans cultivated this about 5,000 years ago, it's thought to be, for seed in the Southwest of the US, which is where I live. So that's pretty cool. What's wild is by about 1550 sunflowers had spread through Europe, through Africa, through Russia. And by 1700 there were early patents for oil extraction methods. In Russia specifically, what was really interesting is it's too hard to grow olives there for olive oil. So sunflowers were grown and cultivated and then pressed for their oil. Let's get into some sunflower types. You have two major sort of split offs. You have the confectionery style, which has the white stripe, the ones that you might eat out of a sunflower seed packet like I did growing up. And then you have the black oil sunflowers, which are commonly grown as sunflowers for microgreens. Those ones contain up to twice as much oil, which is why they have that name. Let's take a closer look at the actual sunflower head itself. It's made of two types of flowers. These ones right here are called the ray flowers. And then inside you have all these tiny little disc flowers, and those are what actually get pollinated and become the sunflower seeds. So when you're growing sunflowers, what you have to know is the growth habits, the growth patterns. What they'll do is they'll throw up vertical growth until they reach what's called their terminal height. About as high as their genetics will let them get to. And that's when they'll throw out the sunflower head. And now here's something that's really surprising about sunflowers. The name and some of the myth around them suggest that they're heliotropic, or they follow the sun. Now that can happen. It seems like it actually does happen, at least in my garden, earlier on in their life when they're younger. But now every single sunflower that I have, every single one unless it's a multiheaded sunflower, is facing east, which tends to be what happens as they mature. So it's actually a little bit awkward because every single one of the sunflowers in my front yard garden is facing east and my front yard garden faces west. So it's like they're too cool for school. But it's kind of an interesting fact, because a lot of the times you'll hear that they just follow the sun no matter what. Here's another fun fact before we get into the growing information. Sunflowers, as you'll find, are pretty easy to grow and they're actually quite good as just a straight up cover crop that adds some beauty to the top of the landscape. But what they do, it's kind of like other plants that are called dynamic accumulators, they go down really really deep, up to four feet deep, in the subsoil. And of course they're taking nutrients, they're taking minerals from the ground and bringing it up into the plant matter. Which then of course, at the end of the season, you use the seeds, do whatever you want with those. And then you can chop and compost or just chop and drop right on top of the surface of the soil. And voila you've brought nutrients up from way down below up to the top to improve your soil. Let's talk now about growing sunflowers and some of the varieties that you can choose. This is the most fun part of the process because you get to really select what the garden's going to look like. I'll put my favorite varieties up in the corner over here. Let's talk about a couple things you want to know before you get to planting. The first one is, there are some that are bred to have no pollen, which is really for the cut flower industry. And you want to avoid those varieties because they're not going to have any beneficial effects for you as far as attracting pollinators. All that kind of stuff is just out the window if you have the no pollen varieties. The other thing you need to know when planting sunflowers is, sometimes they have an inhibitive effect on things like pole beans and potatoes. So you wouldn't want to interplant with those types of crops. But it seems like for anything else you're good to go. Next up is the actual color selection and the size selection. So I, as you can see, have all sorts of different types of sunflowers popped around different spots of the garden. In the tomato trellis back there I used a giant mammoth style with a big yellow classic sunflower head. In the melon patch back there, I believe I have autumn something, I forgot the name. But it's a multiheaded lower grower, great to sprinkle through the middle of a row. But these things come in pink, green, gray, all sorts of different colors. Multicolored, multiheaded. So there's tons and tons of varieties to choose. Let's talk starting seeds for sunflowers. It's actually quite simple. First of all, you don't have to soak a sunflower seed, although it will speed up the germination process. So you can soak it for about four to eight hours or so. That'll let the water get through the seed hull and of course just speed germination up. But again, you don't have to do that. In fact, you don't really have to soak any seed if you don't want to as long as you're keeping your growing medium wet enough for water to get through. So here I have the autumn beauties that are sitting out in my cantaloupe and melon patch. This is a really small sunflower seed. They come in all different shapes, sizes. These ones are quite tiny. But for the most part, the rules are the same. Plant it about an inch deep in your mixture. I've got some seed starting trays here. These are the Epic six cell trays. The thing about it, if you're going to transplant it, is it has a decent sized taproot. Any plant that has a taproot, you don't want to damage the taproot when you're transplanting. Which is why I like the Epic six cells, because they've got that hole at the bottom where the taproot can actually come out undamaged, which is kind of nice. So if you want to, you can plant your seeds pointy side down. Because if you think about when a sunflower seed sprouts, the thicker side of the seed is the side that's on the top that you kind of sometimes have to pull off those two seed leaves. So that's what I would do. When in doubt, you could just plant it sideways and it'll come up how it comes up, not really a big deal. But I will say I would probably be direct sowing these sunflower seeds, if you can. Just because of that taproot thing. You don't want to mess the transplant up. And if you've got the space and you have the time and the conditions are warm enough, direct sow it and you're good to go. Let's assume you have your sunflower growing. It's out in the field and maybe you're running into some problems. The first problem is not enough light. You're gonna want to give it, it's a sunflower guys, six plus hours of sun a day at least I would say. Probably on the eight side, if I were you. The other thing you can run into is wilting. You can see this one's kind of wilting. It's mostly because it's really mature and the seeds are forming and that's heavy and it's pulling it down. But if you see it wilt before this point, good chance you've underwatered it. Another thing you could run into is, what if you don't even have this flowerhead at all, you're not seeing any flowers. Could be too much fertilizer. They don't really want to be pumped full of fertilizer. Remember it's a four foot taproot that's going down. There's plenty of late nutrition in the soil. It's going to do just fine. So don't overfertilize. Another reason for poor flower formation is just it's not light enough. So again, refer back to just giving it more sun. Let's talk now about some pests and problems you might run into. You may run into some mildew issues. You may run into some birds, some caterpillars, some moths. Usually those will want to munch on the foliage. It's not a huge deal. It seems like sunflowers can lose quite a few, especially of the lower leaves, and they're completely fine. Mine often like to shed those when they get to this higher height and they kind of have that terminal flower. It seems like they kind of just say goodbye to the ones down below. But if you are going to use the sunflowers to save the seeds for next season or to eat the seeds, which I'm going to do with this head right here, then you can run into a couple other pests. First of all, the larva of any of the ones I just talked about, can come in and eat. But then there's the sunflower moth which, as the name implies, likes to target these seeds. The larva will come through and just bore right through them. So you're gonna have to handpick those off or spray the actual plant with BT, which is a really, really good organic pesticide for dealing with any sort of larval form of pest. But if you want to, that's what I would do, otherwise just hand pick them off. So you've grown your sunflower, it has a beautiful head and there some delicious seeds and you want to know when to harvest them. Remember, we talked about the ray flowers on the outside of the sunflower, as well as the disc flowers on the inside. When the ray flowers have fallen off, that's your first signal that you're getting close to harvest. But then what you want to do is look at the back of the head of the sunflower. It should start turning yellow, it'll turn even more yellow. And then the ribs of that back will start to turn green to brown. And that's when you know that it's fully ready to harvest. What you do at that point is just snip the head off, let it dry in a cool, well ventilated space. You can let it dry on the plant too, but it just kind of wastes space. So you might want to rip the plants out, chop up the stalks, put those in your compost and then cut the head off and put it somewhere else. And if you want to see me actually take that head that I just showed you and eat the entire thing before the seeds are actually ready - this is something I recently learned that you can do. I'm going to eat the entire sunflower head over on the Epic Homesteading channel, including the recipe. So go Subscribe to that one if you're interested in seeing that. Hopefully this gave you the tips you need to grow sunflowers. Good luck in the garden and keep on growing.

Operating autonomously from low Earth orbit or LEO, the Ark can deliver critical payments to almost any location on Earth within an hour. Engineered for reusability and precision, the Ark utilizes advanced hypersonic maneuvering and robust landing systems, enabling safe runway independent touchdowns on both land and water. Its compact payload bay is ideal for missionritical items such as medical supplies, drones, and communication equipment, making it perfect for defense, emergency, or research operations. The vehicle's aerodynamic structure is built with flaps, thrusters, and advanced heat shielding to survive extreme re-entry conditions and maintain control while traveling at speeds exceeding Mach 20. Furthermore, the Ark acts as a valuable hypersonic test platform for realworld simulations. The Blue Origin Mars Telecommunications or MTO is engineered to revolutionize data exchange between Mars and Earth, establishing a critical dedicated relay hub for future missions. Built upon the Blue Ring modular satellite platform, the MTO features advanced communication systems capable of continuous high bandwidth data transfer connecting Martian orbiters, surface assets, and Earth mission control. To ensure comprehensive coverage, the MTO uses multiple steerable high rate antennas and a broadbeam transmitter supported by smaller UHF relay satellites to link up with surface rovers and landers. Its flexible mission profile is enabled by a hybrid propulsion system, solar, electric, and chemical thrusters for enhanced maneuvering. Designed for long operational life and adaptability, the NTO incorporates onboard processing and AI assisted data management for autonomous real-time routing, making it a foundational element for a stable, scalable Mars communication network, supporting both robotic and future human exploration. As part of NASA's commercial lunar payload services, or CLPS program, this durable and precise lander is designed to deliver scientific and technological payloads directly to the moon's surface. The Blue Ghost is optimized to handle every mission phase from Earth departure and lunar orbit to surface operations. Its robust systems manage communication, power, and thermal control effectively. The lander solar arrays generate over 400 watts of power, supporting instruments that investigate critical areas like dust behavior, surface composition, radiation resistance, and advanced navigation tech. Equipped with a reliable propulsion system for a smooth, soft landing, and rugged thermal regulation to withstand the moon's extreme temperatures, Blue Ghost ensures mission success. The Sunbird nuclear fusion rocket from Pulsar Fusion is a revolutionary fusion propulsion concept engineered to transform interplanetary travel, functioning as an orbital fusion tug, Sunbird would dock with spacecraft in low Earth orbit and provide the necessary thrust for deep space missions. This pioneering system uses a dual direct fusion drive to simultaneously handle both propulsion and onboard power generation within a compact unit. It is designed to produce roughly 2 megawatts of output, delivering both powerful acceleration and electrical power for sophisticated payloads. With a high projected specific impulse of 10,000 to 15,000 seconds, Sunbird could propel a 1,000 kg spacecraft to distant locations like Pluto in less than 5 years. This fusion-driven approach eliminates the limitations of solar power, allowing for robust operation and communication far from the sun. Pulsar fusion plans to validate the core technology through ground and subsequent orbital testing. The DALT Vortex represents France's highly ambitious initiative to develop a fully reusable space plane for both civil and defense operations. This vehicle is engineered to merge aircraft precision with spacecraft capability, allowing it to perform orbital missions, including launching payloads, servicing satellites, or performing inorbit operations before executing an atmospheric re-entry and landing autonomously on a standard runway. The program is structured around a phase development road map. It begins with the Vortex D, a 1/3 scale demonstrator designed specifically to test critical technologies like hypersonic re-entry, advanced flight control, and thermal protection systems. Subsequent iterations known as Vortex S, C, and M will incrementally lead toward full cargo and crude configurations. The Vortex's modular architecture is key to its versatility, enabling orbital servicing, asset retrieval, and rapid redeployment. Backed by France's Defense Ministry and the European Space Agency, the Vortex aims to set a new standard for sustainable multi-m mission European Space Access by significantly reducing launch costs. The European Space Ay's future space transport ecosystem is a visionary plan for Europe's operations in space. Developed under its future launchers preparatory program, this framework prioritizes a connected, reusable, and sustainable approach, the system integrates several core elements. Reusable launch vehicles, orbital refueling, space tugs, and standardized docking interfaces. This integrated approach is designed to enable continuous, flexible access to orbit and beyond. A key objective is establishing modular logistics, allowing spacecraft to refuel, exchange payloads, and extend mission life without relying on single-use launch systems. This supports programs focused on propulsion efficiency like thrust, best and first drive, booster recovery, and disruptive technology development, forming the backbone for future reusable launches. At the center of this ecosystem is Odyssey, an ambitious orbital service and refueling depot. Odyssey is designed to store propellant, maintain satellites, and act as a staging post for missions heading to the moon, Mars, or deep space. By merging innovation and reusability, ISA aims to significantly cut costs, boost mission cadence, and establish Europe as a leader in orbital logistics and sustainable space transport. The Rocket Lab Neutron is a next generation medium lift reusable rocket designed to boost efficiency and reliability in orbital launch services. Built to support commercial, defense, and scientific missions, Neutron can carry up to 13,000 kilograms to low Earth orbit, LEO, while fully recovering its first stage for reuse. Standing approximately 43 m tall, the rocket is powered by Archimedes engines using a high-performance, clean burning combination of liquid oxygen and methane across both stages. Neutron features a lightweight, strong composite structure that ensures excellent aerodynamics and supports rapid mission turnaround. Its advanced recovery system allows for precise landings either back at the launch site or on a dedicated ocean platform named return on investment ROI, providing flexibility for diverse mission profiles. Neutron will be a key asset for deploying satellite constellations, delivering cargo, and supporting national security and advanced government logistics programs. The ADAS J mission developed by Astroskll Japan in partnership with JAXA under the CRD2 program represents a major step toward active orbital debris removal. Its core objective was to demonstrate the complex technology required to approach and inspect a large uncooperative object, a defunct H2A rocket upper stage orbiting Earth. To achieve this, ARSJ is equipped with advanced sensors, highresolution cameras, and sophisticated navigation algorithms, including angles only navigation, and model matching navigation. These tools enabled the spacecraft to precisely detect, track, and analyze a tumbling motion of the debris. During its flight, Adra J successfully executed flyaround observations within 50 m, followed by a close approach of approximately 15 m before an autonomous safety abort. These precise maneuvers validated Astroscale's capability for safe proximity operations, laying the groundwork for the future ADAS J2 mission, which is slated to attempt the physical removal of the debris. The Atmos Phoenix capsule is a novel re-entry system designed to safely return up to 100 kg of cargo from low Earth orbit. Its core innovation is an inflatable atmospheric decelerator and inflatable heat shield that expands during re-entry. This process effectively reduces speed and minimizes thermal stress, safeguarding valuable payloads like material science experiments, biological samples, and in orbit technology tests. The systems viability was proven aboard SpaceX's Bandwagon 3 mission with the Phoenix 1 prototype. Though not designed for recovery, the mission successfully demonstrated heat shield inflation under real re-entry conditions and transmitted crucial telemetry data, validating all major systems. The upcoming Fenix 2 iteration will feature onboard propulsion for controlled descent, an increased payload capacity, and enhanced communication. Through the Phoenix capsule, Atmos aims to provide Europe's first reliable orbital return service, significantly boosting scientific and industrial access to the microgravity environment. The Blue Origin New Glenn is a heavy list orbital rocket designed for maximum reusability, performance, and high volume missions. Towering at 98 meters tall, it features an industry-leading 7- meter wide payload fairing, providing immense internal volume for large satellites and constellation deployments. Its core innovation lies in the reusable first stage, powered by seven BE4 engines that run a liquid oxygen and liqufied natural gas, LOX LNG, for efficient eye thrust performance. This booster is engineered to fly up to 25 missions and performs controlled landings onto a sea-based platform about 1,000 km downrange, utilizing aerodynamic strakes, fins, and deployable landing legs. The upper stage optimized for the vacuum of space uses two BE3 UU engines LOX HL2 and is capable of multiple restarts. This allows it to place payloads into challenging orbits like geostationary transfer orbit GTO or medium earth orbit MEO with a payload capacity of approximately 45 metric tons to low Earth orbit LEO. New Glenn is central to Blue Origin's vision for scalable costefficient space access. Honda's reusable rocket prototype. Honda R&D has made a significant shift from automotive engineering to aerospace with its experimental reusable rocket. This project is designed to prove fundamental capabilities, vertical takeoff, stable flight, and precise vertical landing. The rocket leverages Honda's long-standing expertise in combustion control, propulsion, and autonomous systems, integrating control algorithms derived from its vehicle technology to ensure reliability and reusability. The prototype stands 6.3 m tall and 85 cm wide with a fully fueled weight of approximately 1.3 tons. During a recent test at Honda's Tiki facility in Hokkaido, the rocket demonstrated excellent performance for an early stage prototype. It reached an altitude of 271 m, maintained flight for 56 seconds, and landed within 37 cm of its target. Precision is managed via thrust vectoring control for maneuvering and autonomous landing guidance for recovery. While still in fundamental research, Honda's goal is to achieve suborbital flight by 2029, establishing the company as a credible new force in reusable rocket technology. NASA's commitment to finding life extends far beyond Mars, driving a new vision for a habitable solar system. Central to this effort is defining the habitable zone, the specific orbital region around a star where temperatures allow for stable liquid water on a planet's surface. The cornerstone of this mission is the upcoming habitable world's observatory, HWO. Building on concepts like LUVOR, HWO will use next generation optics and coronagraph technology to directly image faint Earthlike exoplanets. Its primary job will be analyzing your atmospheres for crucial bio signatures like oxygen, methane, and carbon dioxide. Complementing this work, NASA is simultaneously refining climate models to better understand planetary dynamics such as stellar type and tidal locking and conducting missions to study the habitability of moons and smaller worlds within our own solar neighborhood. Polaris Dawn stands as a landmark mission in private space exploration, spearheading SpaceX's advanced commercial human space flight efforts under the leadership of Jared Isacman. Aboard the Crew Dragon Resilience, the fourperson crew, Isacman, Scott Patit, Sarah Gillis, and Anna Menon reached a record-breaking orbital altitude of approximately 1,400 km. This daring trajectory took them through the Van Allen radiation belts to study the effects of radiation on both the crew's health and the spacecraft systems. The mission achieved a major historical first, the world's first commercial extra vehicular activity. The crew tested newlyesed SpaceX EVA suits engineered specifically for the vacuum of space and enhanced mobility. Over the 5-day journey, they conducted essential scientific research, focusing on microgravity's effects on the human body and decompression safety protocols. Furthermore, Polaris successfully tested Starlink's laserbased communication systems, a critical step for future deep space missions. As the debut mission of the Polaris program, it lays a vital foundation for moon and Mars expeditions. SpaceX's Starship represents the most ambitious leap yet in humanity's quest for interplanetary travel. Designed as a fully reusable two-stage rocket system, Starship couples the immense power of the Superheavy booster with a versatile upper stage capable of transporting both crew and cargo across deep space. The mission driven by Elon Musk aims to establish a sustainable human presence on Mars. Initial phases involve sending robotic landers to thoroughly test systems and analyze surface conditions. These early flights will be critical for evaluating atmospheric entry, descent, and landing performance alongside the crucial inorbit refueling process necessary for long duration interplanetary journeys. Subsequent missions will focus on life support trials, habitat deployment, and developing the infrastructure needed for long-term habitation. The ultimate goal is to build a self-sustaining city on Mars through repeated Starship launches during favorable Earth Mars alignment, making this reusable vehicle the cornerstone of our next great frontier. The Jaxa HTVX is Japan's cuttingedge unmanned cargo spacecraft designed to replace the earlier HTV Konotorii vehicle. It excels at its primary mission resupplying the International Space Station ISS while also serving as an extended orbital technology demonstration platform. The HTVX offers significant efficiency gains cargo capacity. It can haul up to 470 kg of pressurized cargo and 1750 kg of unpressurized cargo. Featuring optimized rack layouts for maximum volume. Late loading. A crucial feature is its ability to accept cargo as late as 24 hours before launch, enabling the delivery of time-sensitive experiments and fresh supplies. Duration. The vehicle can remain docked at the ISS for up to 6 months and continue autonomous technology demonstrations in orbit for as long as 18 months after departing the station. Its design incorporates a simplified reliable propulsion subsystem and advanced communication pathways, including ISS links and satellite relays for continuous control. The spacecraft's flexible structure also paves the way for future roles such as docking with other platforms or supporting inspace servicing operations.

- The Boy and the Sunbird 🐦✨ | Animated Read-Aloud Story for Kids | Courage Makes You Shine: Kind: captions Language: en Vion was a quiet boy who loved to watch the world. He often played alone in his garden, a little shy. One sunny morning, a flash of gold caught his eye. A tiny bird, shimmering like a jewel, landed on a flower. It was a sunbird with feathers of bright orange and yellow. The sunbird sang a cheerful tinkling song. Vion watched, mesmerized by its beauty and sound. The sunbird flew a little closer, tilting its head. It seemed to invite Vion to come along. Vienn felt a flutter in his tummy. He was a bit scared to follow, but the sunbird chirped again, a gentle, encouraging sound. Taking a deep breath, Vienn took a small step forward. The sunbird led him to the edge of the tall trees. The forest looked big and a little bit spooky. "Are you afraid?" chirped the sunbird, its voice like tiny bells. Von nodded, his eyes wide. Courage is not the absence of fear, the sunbird sang. But doing it anyway. It flew into the forest, beckoning Von to follow. Von bit his lip, then thought of the sunbird's words. He bravely stepped under the leafy canopy. Inside, the forest was not scary at all. It was filled with soft light and pretty flowers. Squirrels scampered and butterflies danced. The sunbird showed Vian a hidden waterfall, sparkling bright. Vienn splashed his hands in the cool water, laughing. He had never felt so happy and free. The sunbird perched on his shoulder, warm and comforting. See, Vion, it chirped. You were brave. Vion smiled, feeling a new glow inside him. He knew now that being brave meant trying new things. From that day on, Von felt more confident and bright. His courage made him shine. Just like his golden friend.
